text:
A university that offers evening classes to a large number of students who commute to campus wants to estimate the
mean commute time of all evening students. A parking facilities attendant selects one parking lot on campus, then selects
the first 60 students who drive into the lot on a certain evening. Each of the 60 students is asked to provide their typical
commute time to campus. The university will create a confidence interval for the mean commute time of evening students.
Are the conditions for inference satisfied?

options.A: Yes, all conditions for inference are satisfied.
options.B: No, because the distribution of the population mean commute time is not known to be normally distributed.
options.C: No, because the sample size is greater than 10% of the population size.
options.D: No, because the students selected are not a random sample of all evening students.
options.E:
No, because the sample size of 60 is not large enough to assume that the sampling distribution of the sample mean
commute time is approximately normal.

text:
The distribution of the length of employment for workers at a factory is moderately skewed to the right, with mean 7.4
years and standard deviation 5.6 years. The factory manager will select a random sample of 100 employees to survey.
Which of the following best describes the sampling distribution of the sample means of length of employment for samples
of size 100?

options.A: Moderately skewed to the right, with mean 7.4 years and standard deviation 5.6 years
options.B: Moderately skewed to the right, with mean 7.4 years and standard deviation less than 5.6 years
options.C: Moderately skewed to the right, with mean less than 7.4 years and standard deviation less than 5.6 years
options.D: Approximately normal, with mean less than 7.4 years and standard deviation 5.6 years
options.E:
Approximately normal, with mean 7.4 years and standard deviation less than 5.6 years
3

text:
Researchers from a national research organization investigated which platform (television, social media, newspaper, In-
ternet, etc.) United States adults prefer to use to obtain their news. In 2016, of the 200 randomly selected United States
adults that were surveyed, 114 selected television as their preferred platform. In 2018, of the 200 randomly selected
United States adults that were surveyed, 98 selected television as their preferred platform. The conditions for inference
are met. A 95 percent confidence interval for the difference in the population proportions of United States adults that
use television as their preferred platform for obtaining news between 2016 and 2018 (2016 minus 2018) was found to be
(−0.02,0.18).
Based on the interval, is it reasonable for the researchers to claim that there is convincing statistical evidence that the
population proportion of United States adults that use television as their preferred method to obtain news differed between
2016 and 2018?

options.A: No, because the researchers should have used a greater confidence level to obtain convincing evidence.
options.B:
No, because zero is included in the interval there is not convincing statistical evidence that the population proportion
differed between 2016 and 2018.
options.C:
Yes, because zero is included in the interval there is convincing statistical evidence that the population proportion
differed between 2016 and 2018.
options.D:
Yes, because most of the interval is positive there is convincing statistical evidence that the population proportion
increased from 2016 to 2018.
options.E:
Yes, because 0.57 is greater than 0.49 there is convincing statistical evidence that the population proportion decreased
from 2016 to 2018.

text:
A solar power engineer took a random sample of houses and installed the same type of solar panels using two different
methods on each house to investigate whether there is a mean difference in the angles of installation between the two
methods for all houses in the population of interest. The engineer found the sample mean difference between the two
methods to be 0.2 degree and the p-value for a two-sided matched-pairs t-test for the mean difference to be 0.65.
Assuming the conditions for inference are met, which of the following statements is the best interpretation of the p-value?

options.A: The probability that the null hypothesis is true is 0.65.
options.B: If the null hypothesis is true, the probability is 0.65 of observing a mean difference of 0.2 degree or −0.2 degree.
options.C:
If the null hypothesis is true, the probability is 0.65 of observing a mean difference of greater than 0.2 degree or less
than −0.2 degree.
options.D: If the null hypothesis is true, the probability is 0.65 of observing a mean difference of greater than 0.2 degree.
options.E:
If the null hypothesis is true, the probability is 0.65 of observing a mean difference of less than −0.2 degree.
4

The following table shows the classification of all 51 dogs from an animal shelter by whether the dogs lived mostly
outdoors or mostly indoors before coming to the shelter and whether they tested positive or negative for a certain skin
condition.

One dog from the animal shelter will be selected at random. Based on the table, which of the following statements is
correct?

options.A: The probability of selecting a dog that tested positive is greater than 0.5.

options.C:

text:
Researchers are examining the relationship between hours of sleep and athletic performance among college athletes.
Athletic performance will be measured on a numeric scale, with greater numbers indicating better performance. The
researchers expect that the more hours the athletes sleep, the better they will perform. Assuming all conditions for
inference are met, the researchers will create a 95 percent confidence interval for the slope of the regression line for
predicting athletic performance from amount of sleep.
For which of the following would the confidence interval support the researchers’ expectations?

options.A: The confidence interval includes only positive values.
options.B: The confidence interval includes only negative values.
options.C: The confidence interval has a width less than 1.
options.D: The confidence interval has a width greater than 1.
options.E: The confidence interval includes the value 0.

text:
A company that sells baby food is interested in the baby food preferences of all families with toddlers from a certain city.
A representative from the company sets up a booth at one grocery store in the city that will be used to investigate baby
food preference. The representative will ask people with toddlers who walk past the booth to complete the survey.
Which of the following best describes the sampling method the company will use?

options.A: Systematic random sampling
options.B: Simple random sampling
options.C: Cluster sampling
options.D: Stratified random sampling
options.E: Convenience sampling

text:
A large online retailer places packed and ready-to-be-shipped boxes in a line on a conveyor belt. A sample of boxes will
be used to estimate the mean weight of boxes shipped on a given day. One proposed method for selecting the sample is as
follows:
• Generate a random integer from 1 to 20.
• Use that integer to select an initial box from the first 20 boxes in line on the conveyor belt.
• Select every 25th box that follows the initial box on the conveyor belt for the rest of the day.
Which of the following best describes the proposed sampling method?

options.A: Stratified random sample, stratified by time of day
options.B: Cluster sample, with times of day as clusters
options.C: Cluster sample, with box weights as clusters
options.D: Systematic random sample
options.E:
Convenience sample
6

expected value of W?

In a study researching how donating to charity can affect a person’s happiness, 96 participants were given $5 a day for
one week. Each participant was randomly assigned to one of two groups. Those assigned to the first group were asked to
spend the money on themselves, and those assigned to the second group were asked to donate the money to charity. At
the end of the week, all of the participants were asked to rate their overall level of happiness on a scale from 0 to 100, with
higher scores indicating greater levels of happiness.
Which of the following are the appropriate null and alternative hypotheses to test whether the sample data provide con-
vincing statistical evidence that donating to charity results in greater happiness than spending on oneself, on average?

options.A:
H0: µself −µcharity = 0
Ha: µself −µcharity > 0
options.B:
H0: µself −µcharity = 0
Ha: µself −µcharity < 0
options.C:
H0: µself −µcharity = 0
Ha: µself −µcharity ̸= 0
options.D:
H0: µself −µcharity < 0
Ha: µself −µcharity = 0
options.E:
H0: µself −µcharity > 0
Ha: µself −µcharity < 0

text:
Suppose a hypothesis test will be used to investigate whether the proportion of United States adults who have been a
victim of a cybercrime, such as identity theft, is greater than 0.25. Suppose that the hypothesis test is conducted using
a significance level of 0.05 and the power of the test is determined for a specific alternative value using the significance
level of 0.05.
If the significance level of the hypothesis test is changed to 0.01 and the power of the test is computed for the same
alternative value using a significance level of 0.01, which of the following best describes the change(s) to the probability
of a Type I error and the power of the test?

options.A: The probability of a Type I error would decrease, and the power of the test would stay the same.
options.B: The probability of a Type I error would decrease, and the power of the test would increase.
options.C: The probability of a Type I error would decrease, and the power of the test would decrease.
options.D: The probability of a Type I error would increase, and the power of the test would increase.
options.E:
The probability of a Type I error would increase, and the power of the test would decrease.
8

text:
A large company has operated assuming that the rate of absenteeism is the same for each day of the week. The new human
resources director at the company claims that the assumption is false and that the rate of absenteeism is different for some
days of the week as compared with others. To test the claim, a random sample of 150 absences was selected, and the day
of the week on which the absence occurred was recorded. The counts are shown in the following table.
Monday
Tuesday
Wednesday
Thursday
Friday
Number of Absences
40
27
21
34
28
The appropriate chi-square test will be performed to test the claim.
What is the contribution of the Monday absences to the calculation of the chi-square test statistic?

The manager of a restaurant plans to survey a random sample of customers to estimate the proportion of customers who
would return within a week if given a coupon for a free dessert to use on their next visit. A similar coupon was given
last year, and 30 percent of the customers who were given the coupon returned within a week. The manager will use the
observed proportion from last year as a preliminary estimate of the population proportion and wants the margin of error
to be at most 0.04 at a 90 percent level of confidence.
Of the following, which is the least number of customers who should be surveyed such that the estimate of the margin of
error of the 90 percent confidence interval will be no greater than 0.04?

An experiment was conducted to investigate whether submersion in cold water causes a lower heart rate. The experiment
used 50 volunteers. The 25 youngest volunteers had their heart rate measured while holding their breath for 30 seconds
with their face submerged in cold water. The 25 oldest volunteers had their heart rate measured while holding their
breath for 30 seconds with their face not submerged in cold water. The mean heart rate for volunteers who had their face
submerged in cold water was lower than the mean heart rate for volunteers who did not have their face submerged in cold
water.
Which of the following elements of a well-designed experiment is missing?
I. Comparison of at least two treatment groups
II. Random assignment of treatments to experimental units
III. Replication

options.A: I only
options.B: II only
options.C: I and II only
options.D: II and III only
options.E:
I, II, and III
10

text:
A sample of 15 golfers who played a golf course on a certain day was selected. For each golfer, the average driving
distance (x), in yards, and the percent of fairways hit on the drive (y) were recorded.
The scatterplot displays the percent of fairways hit versus the average driving distance. Also shown is the least-squares
regression line, ˆy = 66.228+0.0002x.
The point circled on the scatterplot is considered an influential point. A new least-squares regression line will be calculated
with the influential point removed. How will the removal of the influential point affect the new least-squares regression
line for the remaining 14 points?

options.A: The y-intercept will remain the same, and the slope will be negative.
options.B: The y-intercept will decrease, and the slope will be negative.
options.C: The y-intercept will decrease, and the slope will be positive.
options.D: The y-intercept will increase, and the slope will be negative.
options.E: The y-intercept will increase, and the slope will be positive.

text:
For the population of people in the United States who are at least 18 years of age, approximately 61 percent voted in a
recent election. A random sample of 200 people and a random sample of 100 people will be selected from the population.
For which sample size is it more likely that greater than 70 percent of the sample voted in the recent election?

options.A: They are equally likely, because the mean of the sampling distribution is the same for both sample sizes.
options.B:
It is more likely for a sample size of 200 people, because there is more variability in the sampling distribution for
larger sample sizes.
options.C:
It is more likely for a sample size of 200 people, because there is less variability in the sampling distribution for
larger sample sizes.
options.D:
It is more likely for a sample of size 100 people, because there is more variability in the sampling distribution for
smaller sample sizes.
options.E:
It is more likely for a sample size of 100 people, because there is less variability in the sampling distribution for
smaller sample sizes.
11

text: The probability that a particular electrical component operates successfully is 3

An electrical system consists of three
such components that operate independently. The system will function successfully if at least one of the three components
operates successfully.
What is the probability that the system will function successfully?

A food scientist conducting a study to investigate the nutritional habits of teenagers in a city selected a random sample
of 100 teenagers in the city. Each of the 100 teenagers was asked to complete a survey with questions about age and the
number of ounces of carbonated drinks consumed on the previous day.
Assuming the conditions for inference have been met, which of the following is an appropriate test and alternative hy-
pothesis to use to investigate whether the average number of ounces of carbonated drinks consumed in a day increases
with age for teenagers in the city?

options.A: A t-test for the slope of a regression model with alternative hypothesis: Ha: β > 0
options.B: A t-test for the slope of a regression model with alternative hypothesis: Ha: β < 0
options.C: A t-test for the slope of a regression model with alternative hypothesis: Ha: β = 0
options.D: A one-sample z-test for a population proportion with alternative hypothesis: Ha: p > 0.5
options.E: A two-sample t-test for a difference of two population means with alternative hypothesis: Ha: µolder ̸= µyounger

text:
For a random sample of 50 newborn Holstein calves from the United States, the mean weight was 80 pounds and the
standard deviation was 6 pounds. In order to compare these findings with those of other countries, an agricultural engineer
converted the measured calf weights from pounds to kilograms by multiplying each weight by 0.454.
What are the mean and standard deviation of the converted weights of the 50 newborn Holstein calves?

options.A: The mean weight is 36.32 kilograms and the standard deviation is 2.724 kilograms.
options.B: The mean weight is 36.32 kilograms and the standard deviation is 6 kilograms.
options.C: The mean weight is 80 kilograms and the standard deviation is 2.724 kilograms.
options.D: The mean weight is 80 kilograms and the standard deviation is 6 kilograms.
options.E:
The mean weight is approximately 176.21 kilograms and the standard deviation is 6 kilograms.
12

text:
The distribution of housing prices in a city includes several outliers at the upper end of the distribution and no outliers at
the lower end of the distribution.
Which of the following is the most resistant, or robust, measure of center of the distribution of housing prices?

options.A: The mean, because it is affected by the outliers.
options.B: The mean, because it is not greatly affected by the outliers.
options.C: The median, because it is affected by the outliers.
options.D: The median, because it is not greatly affected by the outliers.
options.E: The mode, because the data are categorical.

text:
A study reported that 14 percent of cars registered in 2015 had a manual transmission. An automotive researcher collected
data on cars registered in 2021 to investigate whether the proportion of registered cars with a manual transmission is less
than 0.14. The researcher took a random sample of 150 cars registered in 2021 and found 18 of the cars had a manual
transmission.
After ensuring the conditions for inference were met, the researcher conducted an appropriate hypothesis test and calcu-
lated a p-value of 0.24. At the 5 percent level of significance, which of the following conclusions should be made?

options.A:
The null hypothesis should be rejected. There is significant evidence that the population proportion of registered
cars with a manual transmission in 2021 is less than 0.14.
options.B:
The null hypothesis should be rejected. There is not significant evidence that the population proportion of registered
cars with a manual transmission in 2021 is less than 0.14.
options.C:
The null hypothesis should not be rejected. There is significant evidence that the population proportion of registered
cars with a manual transmission in 2021 is not less than 0.14.
options.D:
The null hypothesis should not be rejected. There is significant evidence that the population proportion of registered
cars with a manual transmission in 2021 is equal to 0.14.
options.E:
The null hypothesis should not be rejected. There is not significant evidence that the population proportion of
registered cars with a manual transmission in 2021 is less than 0.14.

text: For which of the following distributions is the proportion of values between 900 and 1,000 the greatest?

options.A: A normal distribution with mean 900 and standard deviation 100
options.B: A normal distribution with mean 950 and standard deviation 100
options.C: A normal distribution with mean 800 and standard deviation 100
options.D: A normal distribution with mean 1,000 and standard deviation 400
options.E:
A normal distribution with mean 950 and standard deviation 50
13

Which of the following is an appropriate interpretation of the 95 percent confidence interval?

options.A:
We are 95% confident that the purchase amount from a randomly selected online store sale will be between $11.55
and $19.05 greater than the purchase amount from a randomly selected retail store sale.
options.B:
We are 95% confident that the sample mean purchase amount from the online store is between $11.55 and $19.05

text:
A paint manufacturer conducted an experiment to investigate whether a new formula produces paint that lasts longer on
the exterior of a building than paint produced using an older formula. A grid of 20 squares was drawn on one side of an
exterior wall of a building. The new formula was randomly assigned to 10 squares, and the older formula was assigned to
the remaining 10 squares. Each square was painted with the assigned formula. One year later, each square was rated on
how well the paint had lasted, using a numerical scale for which larger values indicated longer-lasting paint.
Which of the following best describes the design of the experiment?

options.A: A completely randomized design, because the two formulas were compared.
options.B: A completely randomized design, because each formula was randomly assigned to 10 squares.
options.C: A matched pairs design, because the same number of squares were assigned to each formula.
options.D: A matched pairs design, because two formulas were compared.
options.E: A randomized block design, because the wall was partitioned into 20 squares.

text:
A 95 percent confidence interval for the proportion of parents who use parental controls for blocking, filtering, or moni-
toring their teenagers’ online activities is (0.36,0.42).
Which of the following could be a 99 percent confidence interval based on the same data?

Luisa is a manager at a pet insurance company with many customers. Sixty percent of the company’s customers filed
claims in the past year. Luisa will randomly select 12 customers from all the company’s customers.
Assuming the customers selected are independent of each other, which of the following is closest to the probability that
more than 2 of the 12 customers sampled did not file claims in the past year?

A veterinarian interested in pet nutrition surveyed owners of a single pet (either a dog, a cat, or a ferret) to ask whether
they feed their pet primarily dry pet food, canned pet food, or home-cooked food. The following segmented bar graph
shows the distribution of the three types of food for each type of pet.
Which of the following statements about pet owners in the survey is supported by the segmented bar graph?

options.A: Of all owners who fed their pet primarily dry pet food, the least number owned a cat.
options.B: More than half of the owners fed their pet primarily canned pet food.
options.C: Ninety percent of owners fed their pets primarily dry pet food.
options.D:
The number of ferret owners who fed their pets primarily dry pet food was equal to the number of ferret owners who
fed their pets primarily home-cooked food.
options.E:
A majority of dog owners fed their pets primarily canned pet food.
17

text:
The boxplot summarizes the reading rates, in words per minute (wpm), for 160 elementary school students.
Based on the boxplot, which of the following statements is not true for the data set?

options.A: The data set contains two outliers.
options.B: The range is greater than 30 wpm.
options.C: At least half of the students had reading rates of at most 56 wpm.
options.D: More students had reading rates between 30 wpm and 52 wpm than between 52 wpm and 70 wpm.
options.E:
More students had reading rates greater than 52 wpm than had reading rates less than 56 wpm.

needed. A science teacher decided to investigate whether some students are more likely to ask for help than others. The
teacher randomly selected 200 students from a Biology II course and gave them directions for a short lab procedure that
were purposefully unclear. For each student, the teacher recorded whether the student asked for clarification and the grade
the student earned, A, B, or C, in the preceding Biology I course. Results are listed in the following table.

options.A: Yes, because the p-value is greater than 0.05.

text:
The tail length of Siberian tigers is approximately normally distributed with a mean of 0.85 meter and a standard deviation
of 0.13 meter.
Which of the following is the best interpretation of the z-score for a particular Siberian tiger with a tail length of 0.8 meter?

options.A: The tiger’s tail length is approximately 0.38 standard deviation below the mean.
options.B: The tiger’s tail length is approximately 0.38 standard deviation above the mean.
options.C: The tiger has an approximate 0.38 probability of having a tail length of 0.8 meter.
options.D: The tiger’s tail length is approximately 0.0065 meter greater than the standard deviation.
options.E:
The tiger’s tail length is approximately 0.05 meter below the mean.
19

text:
A common measure of flexibility is the sit-and-reach test, where a person sits on the floor with legs straight in front.
The person then reaches as far forward as possible, and the distance reached past the feet is recorded. The histograms
summarize the results of the sit-and-reach test for two groups, young adult men and middle-aged men. Negative distances
represent a reach that is not as far as the feet.
Which of the following statements is the best description of the distributions of distances?

options.A:
The distribution for young adult men is approximately symmetric, and the distribution for middle-aged men is
skewed to the right.
options.B:
The distribution for young adult men is approximately symmetric, and the distribution for middle-aged men is
skewed to the left.
options.C:
The distribution for young adult men is skewed to the right, and the distribution for middle-aged men is approxi-
mately symmetric.
options.D:
The distribution for young adult men is skewed to the left, and the distribution for middle-aged men is approximately
symmetric.
options.E:
The distribution for young adult men is skewed to the left, and the distribution for middle-aged men is skewed to the
right.

investigation.

affecting the fish that live there.

temperature along all locations of the river has a distribution with mean 18°C and standard deviation of 2°C.

random variable F represent the mean water temperature, in degrees Celsius, of 64 locations along the river selected at

by the water district is likely to yield a sample mean water temperature different from the sample mean water

• Shape:
◦Component 1 cannot be satisfied if a response describes the stem-and-leaf plot as unimodal or normal or
approximately normal.
◦A response that only refers to a gap does not satisfy component 1.
◦A response that refers to a “dip” near 362 satisfies the bimodal shape component.
◦A response that addresses the center or spread of each of the two clusters satisfies component 1.
◦A response that addresses only symmetry, while appropriate, does not satisfy component 1.
• Center:
◦A response that addresses center using interval language such as “the mean of the distribution is
between 359 and 365” must, for any single measure of center, provide an interval with a lower endpoint
no less than 359

whether the difference in note-taking methods has an impact on student quiz performance and is considering the

An appropriate inference procedure is a two-sample t-test for a difference of two population means.
Scoring
Section 1 is scored as follows:
Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following three components:
States a correct null hypothesis of equal means AND a correct one-sided alternative hypothesis
Provides sufficient context by including references to:
• the two groups (types of diets; Diet J and Diet W)
• populations (all overweight large-breed dogs similar to those in the study)
• the response variable (weight loss)
Identifies a two-sample t-test for a difference in population means by name
(e.g., “two-sample t-test”) or by formula
Partially correct (P) if the response does not meet the criteria for E but satisfies either component 1 and/or component 3.
Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.
Additional Notes:
• The elements of component 2 do not have to be satisfied with the statement of the hypotheses. They may be
satisfied by work presented anywhere in the response, most likely by the statement of the conclusion.

provide convincing statistical evidence that for dogs similar to those used in the study that the mean weight loss for

Part (d):
Yes, based on the simulation analysis of sums of ranks of differences, it is reasonable to conclude that not listening to
music tends to result in longer running times. The observed value of the test statistic S is 40, and the simulation analysis
reveals that obtaining a test statistic value at least this large only occurred in 25 of the 1,000 simulated trials that were
carried out under the assumption that music has no effect on running time. Because this indicates an unlikely event,
it is reasonable to conclude that not listening to music tends to result in longer running times.

Students in an online course are each randomly assigned to receive either standard practice exercises or adaptive practice
exercises. For the adaptive practice exercises, the next question asked is determined by whether the student got the
previous question correct. The teacher of the course wants to determine whether there is a difference between the two
practice exercise types by comparing the proportion of students who pass the course from each group. The teacher plans
to test the null hypothesis pS = pA that versus the alternative hypothesis pS ̸= pA, where pS represents the proportion of
students who would pass the course using standard practice exercises and pA represents the proportion of students who
would pass the course using adaptive practice exercises.
The teacher knows that the 99 percent confidence interval for the difference in proportion of students passing the course
for the two practice exercise types (standard minus adaptive) is (0.03,0.24) and the 90 percent confidence interval for the
difference in the proportion of students passing the course for the two practice exercise types (standard minus adaptive)
is (0.06,0.21). Which of the following is the most reasonable conclusion for the teacher’s hypothesis test at the α = 0.05
level?

options.A:
The p-value is less than 0.01, so there is convincing statistical evidence that the proportion of students who would
pass the course for the two practice exercise types differs at the 5% level.
options.B:
The p-value is between 0.01 and 0.10, so there is convincing statistical evidence that the proportion of students who
would pass the course for the two practice exercise types differs at the 5% level.
options.C:
The p-value is between 0.01 and 0.10, so the researchers cannot determine whether the proportion of students who
would pass the course for the two practice exercise types differs at the 5% level.
options.D:
The p-value is between 0.01 and 0.10, so there is not convincing statistical evidence that the proportion of students
who would pass the course for the two practice exercise types differs at the 5% level.
options.E:
The p-value is greater than 0.10, so there is not convincing statistical evidence that the proportion of students who
would pass the course for the two practice exercise types differs at the 5% level.

bread dough and a large batch of whole wheat bread dough were prepared. Each of the batches was formed into 18 loaves,

text:
A chocolatier, a person who makes chocolate candy, at a candy company has developed a new dark chocolate candy and
believes it will be less bitter than the current dark chocolate candy on the market. The chocolatier hired 40 professional
taste testers to participate in an experiment. The chocolatier flipped a coin for each participant. If the coin landed on
heads, the participant tasted the new dark chocolate candy first, then tasted the current dark chocolate candy. If the coin
landed on tails, the participant tasted the current dark chocolate candy first, then tasted the new dark chocolate candy. The
participants rated the bitterness of each dark chocolate candy on a scale of 1 to 10, with 1 being the least bitter and 10
being the most bitter. Using a paired t-test to test the belief, the chocolatier calculated a p-value of 0.274.
Assuming the conditions for inference have been met, which of the following is the best conclusion for the chocolatier’s
hypothesis test?

options.A:
Because the p-value is less than any reasonable level of significance, there is convincing statistical evidence to
support the belief that the new dark chocolate candy is less bitter than the current dark chocolate candy on average
for those similar to the participants in the study.
options.B:
Because the p-value is less than any reasonable level of significance, there is not convincing statistical evidence to
support the belief that the new dark chocolate candy is less bitter than the current dark chocolate candy on average
for those similar to the participants in the study.
options.C:
Because the p-value is greater than any reasonable level of significance, there is convincing statistical evidence to
support the belief that the new dark chocolate candy is less bitter than the current dark chocolate candy on average
for those similar to the participants in the study.
options.D:
Because the p-value is greater than any reasonable level of significance, there is not convincing statistical evidence
to support the belief that the new dark chocolate candy is less bitter than the current dark chocolate candy on average
for those similar to the participants in the study.
options.E:
Since the taste testers were not randomly selected, this was not a well-designed experiment and a conclusion about
the bitterness of the new dark chocolate candy cannot be made.

The distribution of body temperature for healthy adults is approximately normal with mean 98.2◦F and standard deviation
0.7◦F. Based on the description of the distribution, which of the following statements CANNOT be supported?

options.A: More than 95% of healthy adults have a body temperature greater than 96.8◦F.
options.B: The first quartile of the distribution of healthy adults’ body temperature is less than 97.5◦F.
options.C: Approximately 50% of healthy adults have a body temperature greater than 98.2◦F.
options.D: Approximately 95% of healthy adults have a body temperature between 96.8◦F and 99.6◦F.
options.E: A body temperature of 100.3◦F or more would occur in less than 2% of healthy adults.

text:
A research scientist is conducting an experiment to determine whether a new chemical process creates less toxic byproduct
compared to the current chemical process. The scientist took a random sample of products made with the current chemical
process and calculated the sample mean amount of toxic byproduct created. The scientist then took a random sample of
products made with the new chemical process and calculated the sample mean amount of toxic byproduct created. The
difference in the sample means (current minus new) was 2.31 liters of toxic byproduct. A hypothesis test was conducted
using the following hypotheses.
H0: µcurrent −µnew = 0
Ha: µcurrent −µnew ̸= 0
Assuming the conditions for inference were met, the scientist calculated the p-value of the test to be 0.072. Which of the
following statements is the best interpretation of the p-value?

options.A: The probability that the null hypothesis is true is 0.072.
options.B: The probability that the alternative hypothesis is true is 0.072.
options.C: The probability of observing a difference in means of 2.31 liters of toxic byproduct is 0.072.
options.D:
If the null hypothesis is true, the probability of observing a difference in means of at least 2.31 liters of toxic
byproduct is 0.072.
options.E:
If the null hypothesis is true, the probability of observing a difference in means of at most 2.31 liters of toxic
byproduct is 0.072.

text:
For a chi-square test for independence with 6 degrees of freedom and a significance level of 0.05, the value of the test
statistic was χ2 = 10.497. The associated p-value was 0.1052.
Assuming all necessary conditions for the chi-square test were met, what type of error is possible?

options.A: A Type I error is possible, because the null hypothesis was rejected.
options.B: A Type I error is possible, because the null hypothesis was not rejected.
options.C: No error is possible, because the correct decision was made.
options.D: A Type II error is possible, because the null hypothesis was rejected.
options.E: A Type II error is possible, because the null hypothesis was not rejected.

text:
A prize game at a circus has a spinner with 12 sections, where each section is equally likely to be selected when the
spinner is spun. There are 7 blue sections and 5 orange sections. Camille has spun the spinner 4 times and the pattern of
outcomes was orange, blue, orange, blue. Camille will spin the spinner two more times.
Which of the following statements is true?

options.A:
On Camille’s fifth spin of the spinner, the probability that the spinner will land in a blue-colored section is equal to
the probability that the spinner will land in an orange-colored section.
options.B:
On Camille’s fifth spin of the spinner, the probability that the spinner will land in a blue-colored section is less than
the probability that the spinner will land in an orange-colored section.
options.C:
On Camille’s fifth and sixth spins of the spinner, the probability that the spinner will land in a blue-colored section
for both spins is equal to the probability that the spinner will land in an orange-colored section for both spins.
options.D:
On Camille’s fifth and sixth spins of the spinner, the probability that the spinner will land in a blue-colored section
for both spins is greater than the probability that the spinner will land in an orange-colored section for both spins.
options.E:
On Camille’s fifth and sixth spins of the spinner, the probability that the spinner will land in a blue-colored section
for both spins is less than the probability that the spinner will land in an orange-colored section for both spins.

text:
Suppose a new regulation requires businesses to pay all employees a minimum wage of $15.00 per hour. A small coffee
shop currently has 9 employees—5 employees whose wages are less than $15.00 per hour and 4 employees whose wages
are more than $15.00 per hour. To meet the new regulation, the coffee shop plans to increase the wages of the 5 employees
whose wages are less than $15.00 per hour to $15.00 per hour. The other 4 employees will not receive a wage increase at
this time.
Which of the following must be true about the median wage of the 9 employees after the wage increase occurs?

options.A: The median wage will be greater than $15.00 per hour.
options.B: The median wage will increase but will be less than $15.00 per hour.
options.C: The median wage will be $15.00 per hour.
options.D: The median wage will not change.
options.E:
The median wage cannot be determined from the information provided.
7

text:
A technology company has 1,200 employees. The director of human resources at the company selects 250 of the employ-
ees at random, without replacement, and asks them to fill out an anonymous survey asking whether they plan to attend the
company’s holiday party. The proportion of employees in the sample who plan to attend the party is 0.816. Based on this
proportion, the director calculates a 95 percent confidence interval for the proportion of all employees who plan to attend
the holiday party as 0.816±1.96
r
(0.816)(1−0.816)
250
.
Which of the following is true concerning whether it is appropriate to use the confidence interval to estimate the proportion
of all employees who plan to attend the holiday party?

options.A: The confidence interval is appropriate to use because the employees were randomly selected.
options.B: The confidence interval is not appropriate to use because one of the values n ˆp and n(1−ˆp) is less than than 10.
options.C:
The confidence interval is not appropriate to use because 250 employees is more than 10% of all employees at the
company.
options.D: The confidence interval is appropriate to use because n ˆp and n(1−ˆp) are greater than 10.
options.E: The confidence interval is appropriate to use because n > 30.

text:
A total of 13 distinct groups, but with each group similar to the other groups, participated in a craft fair. To investigate
how satisfied the participants were with the fair, the fair director conducted a survey. The director randomly selected 5 of
the groups participating in the fair and surveyed all members of the selected groups.
Which statement best describes the sampling method used by the director?

options.A: It was convenience sampling, because participants from each group were easy to contact.
options.B: It was simple random sampling, because the participants were selected at random.
options.C: It was stratified random sampling, because the director ensured that members from each group were selected.
options.D: It was cluster sampling, because the sample consisted of all members of the 5 randomly selected groups.
options.E: It was systematic random sampling, because the director selected exactly 5 groups.

text:
A grocery store received three shipments of bags of cranberries: one from farm A, one from farm B, and one from farm
C. The distributions of the weight of a bag are unimodal and symmetric for all three shipments. The number of bags and
the mean and standard deviation, in ounces (), for each shipment are shown in the table.
Farm
Number of Bags
Mean (oz)
Standard Deviation (oz)
A
100
16.0
0.5

100
16.0
0.1

100

Which of the following statements is true?

options.A: The shipment from farm A is most likely to have the greatest number of bags that weigh less than 16 oz.
options.B: The shipment from farm B is most likely to have the greatest number of bags that weigh less than 16 oz.
options.C: The shipment from farm C is most likely to have the greatest number of bags that weigh less than 16 oz.
options.D:
The shipments from farms B and C are the only shipments that are likely to have an approximately equal number of
bags that weigh less than 16 oz.
options.E:
The shipments from farms A, B, and C are all likely to have an approximately equal number of bags that weigh less

text:
A certain computer chip company has a machine that makes a large number of computer chips. Of the computer chips
produced on this machine, 3% are defective. Miriam, the quality control inspector, will randomly select computer chips
from the machine to inspect.
What is the probability that the first defective computer chip Miriam finds is the sixth computer chip she inspects from the
machine?

A county manager selected a random sample of 200 residents from a suburban area of the county and a separate random
sample of 200 residents from an urban area of the same county. The residents in the samples were asked whether they
thought the county should create dog parks where residents can take their dogs to play. From those who were sampled,
41 percent of the suburban residents and 35 percent of the urban residents thought the county should create dog parks.
Let pS represent the population proportion of suburban residents who think the county should create dog parks, and let pU
represent the population proportion of urban residents who think the county should create dog parks. The county manager
will test the following hypotheses.
H0: pS −pU = 0
Ha: pS −pU ̸= 0
Assuming the conditions for inference were met, which of the following is closest to the p-value of the county manager’s
test?

A fair coin will be tossed three times. The set of possible outcomes may be described by the ordered triples
{HHH,HHT,HTH,HTT,THH,THT,TTH,TTT}
, where H represents heads and T represents tails.
What is the probability that all three tosses result in heads, given that the three tosses result in more heads than tails?

A researcher wants to examine the effect of 2 different strengths—low and high—of fertilizer on the growth of corn. A
field will be divided into 54 equally sized plots of land, and the 2 fertilizer strengths will be randomly assigned so that
27 plots will be fertilized with low strength and 27 plots with high strength. The same type and amount of corn will be
planted in each plot at the same time. The yield (amount of corn produced) will be measured for each plot.
Which of the following would provide the best evidence that the strength of fertilizer had an effect on the yield?

options.A:
The variation in yields within the fertilizer strength groups is greater than the variation in the average yields between
the fertilizer strength groups.
options.B:
The variation in yields within the fertilizer strength groups is less than the variation in the average yields between
the fertilizer strength groups.
options.C: The variation in yields is about the same for all plots.
options.D:
The variation in yields of plots receiving the low-strength fertilizer is greater than the variation in yields of plots
receiving the high-strength fertilizer.
options.E:
The variation in yields of plots receiving the low-strength fertilizer is less than the variation in yields of plots
receiving the high-strength fertilizer.

According to the United States Social Security Administration (SSA), for children born years ago, the following table
gives the percentage of children with a first name beginning with the letters A–C, D–J, K–M, and N–Z.

of students at her high school who were born 18 years ago is consistent with the percentages from the Social Security
Administration. She took a random sample of 80 students from her school who were born 18 years ago. Daphne recorded
the number of students with a first name beginning with the letters A–C, D–J, K–M, and N–Z. The results are shown in
the following table.

Assuming the conditions for inference are met, what is the appropriate chi-square test and calculation of the test statistic
for her investigation?

To determine whether an herbal supplement helps relieve knee pain, researchers will select 50 patients with knee pain to
participate in a study. The participants will be randomly assigned to one of two groups, with 25 participants in each group.
The first group will receive the herbal supplement daily for one week, and the second group will receive a placebo daily
for one week. Patients will continue to take their current pain medication during the week. At the end of the week, the
patients will record their pain score on a scale of 1 to 10, where 1 represents no pain and 10 represents extreme pain.
Assuming the conditions for inference are met, which of the following would be the most appropriate statistical method
to estimate the difference in mean pain score between the herbal supplement and the placebo for all patients with knee
pain who are similar to the participants in the study?

options.A: A one-sample z-interval for estimating a population proportion
options.B: A one-sample t-interval for estimating a population mean
options.C: A matched-pairs t-interval for estimating a population mean difference
options.D: A two-sample z-interval for estimating a difference between population proportions
options.E: A two-sample t-interval for estimating a difference between population means

text:
It is known that 75 percent of all households in the United States have intermittent Internet connectivity problems.
For 450 random samples of independent households in the United States, what are the mean, µ, and standard deviation,
σ, of the distribution of the number of households that have intermittent Internet connectivity problems?

Attendance records at a large high school show that 6 percent of students are late to school on a typical day. The students
in an AP Statistics class believe the rate is higher than 6 percent. The students took a simple random sample of 75 students
from the school and asked, “Were you late to school today?” In their sample, 6 out of the 75 students, or 8 percent of the
sample, said they were late to school that day. The AP Statistics students conducted a simulation with 100 samples of 75
students from a population where 6 percent of students are late. The results of the simulation are shown in the dotplot.
Based on the simulation and the results of the AP Statistics class’s sample, is there convincing statistical evidence that
more than 6 percent of all students at the school are late to school on a typical day?

options.A: Yes, because 8% of students in their sample said they were late to school that day.
options.B: Yes, because about 47% of the simulated sample percentages of students late to school are 6% or more.
options.C: No, because about 47% of the simulated sample percentages of students late to school are 6% or more.
options.D: Yes, because about 29% of the simulated sample percentages of students late to school are 8% or more.
options.E: No, because about 29% of the simulated sample percentages of students late to school are 8% or more.

text:
The distribution of long jump distance in a statewide high school league is approximately normal with a mean of 18.5
feet and a standard deviation of 3.2 feet. Two students, Joshua and Mateo, from this league competed in the long jump.
Joshua’s distance was 0.52 standard deviation below the mean of the distribution. Mateo’s distance was at the 48th
percentile of the distribution.
At approximately what percentile was the distance jumped by Joshua, and how did his distance compare to Mateo’s
distance?

options.A: Joshua’s distance was at the 30th percentile of the distribution and was less than Mateo’s distance.
options.B: Joshua’s distance was at the 30th percentile of the distribution and was greater than Mateo’s distance.
options.C: Joshua’s distance was at the 48th percentile of the distribution and was less than Mateo’s distance.
options.D: Joshua’s distance was at the 48th percentile of the distribution and was greater than Mateo’s distance.
options.E:
Joshua’s distance was at the 70th percentile of the distribution and was greater than Mateo’s distance.
12

text:
A large manufacturing company has tens of thousands of employees, and 30 percent of the employees have completed a
management training course. Suppose a simple random sample of 50 employees is selected.
Which of the following is closest to the probability that more than half of the sample will be employees who have
completed the management training course?

The recreation committee of a certain town is interested in creating a new nature trail for walkers, runners, and bikers. A
random sample of 100 town residents were asked whether they are in favor of creating the new trail. Of those sampled,
78 said yes.
What is the population of interest for which the committee can generalize its conclusions?

options.A: The 100 town residents who were asked about whether they are in favor of creating the new trail
options.B: The 78 town residents who responded that they are in favor of creating the new trail
options.C: All town residents who are walkers, runners, or bikers
options.D: All town residents who are in favor of the new trail
options.E: All town residents

text:
Carol, a reporter in a large city, took a random sample of 300 households and found that the 90 percent confidence interval
for the proportion of households that own a pet is (0.63,0.71). Jason, the director of a local animal shelter, wants to verify
this result for the proportion of households that own a pet and takes a different random sample of 200 households and
finds that 67 percent own a pet.
Assume the conditions for inference have been met. Jason will calculate a 90 percent confidence interval for the proportion
of households that own a pet using his sample data. How would the margin of error from Jason’s confidence interval
compare to the margin of error from Carol’s confidence interval?

options.A: Jason’s margin of error would be the same as Carol’s margin of error because the confidence level did not change.
options.B: Jason’s margin of error would be the same as Carol’s margin of error because the point estimate did not change.
options.C: Jason’s margin of error would be less than Carol’s margin of error because Jason used a smaller sample size.
options.D: Jason’s margin of error would be greater than Carol’s margin of error because Jason used a smaller sample size.
options.E:
Jason’s margin of error would be greater than Carol’s margin of error because Jason’s point estimate is greater.
13

text:
A botanist is studying the weights, measured in pounds, of pumpkins grown at two different farms. The boxplots show
the distribution of pumpkin weight for two samples of equal size, one from farm A and the other from farm B.
Based on the boxplots, which of the following is a correct conclusion about the distribution of pumpkin weight for the
samples from the two farms?

options.A: The interquartile ranges of the pumpkin weights are the same for farm A and farm B.
options.B: The range of the pumpkin weights for farm B is greater than the range for farm A.
options.C: There are more pumpkin weights that are greater than 18 pounds from farm A than there are from farm B.
options.D: There are more pumpkin weights that are greater than 12 pounds from farm B than there are from farm A.
options.E: There are more pumpkin weights that are less than 10 pounds from farm B than there are from farm A.

text:
Voters in a certain large county will have an opportunity to vote for or against a new environmental rule that would incen-
tivize the return of certain used batteries to a county-certified recycling facility. The results of a previous poll found that
41 percent of registered voters in that county plan to vote for the environmental rule. Juwan, an engineer working at the
recycling facility, randomly selected 15 registered voters and asked whether they plan to vote for the rule.
Assuming that 41 percent represents the actual percent of registered voters in the county who plan to vote for the environ-
mental rule, which of the following is closest to the probability that at most 2 of the registered voters Juwan selected plan
to vote for the environmental rule?

A data scientist had different 5 sets of data. For each data set the scientist fit a least-squares regression line, then graphed
the residuals versus the predicted values for each. Which residual plot indicates that the least-squares regression model
was the most appropriate model to fit the data?

A jewelry manufacturer tracks sales of three types of jewelry (rings, necklaces, and earrings) across each of three differ-
ent retail outlet categories (specialty store, department store, and warehouse club). The segmented bar chart shows the
distribution for each retail outlet for the three jewelry types based on sales data for the past week.
Based on the chart, which statement must be true?

options.A: The percent of earring sales at department stores was less than the percent of necklace sales at specialty stores.
options.B: For each retail outlet category, less than 30% of sales were earrings.
options.C: The number of earring sales for department stores was the same as the number of earring sales for warehouse clubs.
options.D: The greatest number of ring sales came from warehouse clubs.
options.E: The number of ring sales for specialty stores was greater than the number of ring sales for warehouse clubs.

text:
A one-sample z-test for a population proportion was conducted to test H0 : p = 0.35 versus Ha : p ̸= 0.35.
Assuming the conditions for inference were met, which combination of sample proportion and sample size would result
in the smallest p-value of the test?

options.A: ˆp = 0.35 and n = 200
options.B: ˆp = 0.40 and n = 100
options.C: ˆp = 0.40 and n = 60
options.D: ˆp = 0.25 and n = 100

text:
Two scatterplots are shown.
Based on the scatterplots, which statement CANNOT be true?

options.A: Plot A indicates a nonlinear relationship between the variables.
options.B: Plot B indicates a linear relationship between the variables.
options.C: The correlation coefficient for the data in plot B is close to 1.
options.D: The correlation coefficient for the data in plot A is close to 0.
options.E:
The r2 value of the least-squares regression line for plot B is greater than the r2 value of the least-squares regression
line for plot A.

text:
Researchers studying the relationship between screen time and developmental scores of children in Canada selected a
random sample of 2,441 children. For each child, the researchers recorded the child’s screen time, in hours per day, when
they were 2 years old (x) and their scores on a developmental test, in points, when they were 5 years old (y). Higher scores
on the developmental test are better. The 95 percent confidence interval for the slope of the least-squares regression line
for predicting scores on the developmental test based on screen time was (−0.13,−0.02).
The conditions for inference were met, and the researchers concluded that increased screen time is associated with lower
scores on the developmental test. Which of the following provides the best reason that supports the researchers’ conclu-
sion?

options.A: This was a 95% confidence interval, which is a high level of confidence.
options.B: The sample size was large.
options.C: The researchers took years to complete the study.
options.D: The confidence interval for the slope of the regression line does not contain 0.
options.E:
The confidence interval for the slope of the regression line consists entirely of negative numbers.
17

text:
To investigate whether more than half the adults in a country are fearful of riding in a self-driving car, researchers surveyed
a random sample of 1,600 adults in the country. Of those in the sample, 1,280 adults responded that they would be fearful
of riding in a self-driving car.
Which of the following represents the value of the test statistic for the appropriate hypothesis test for the investigation?

Researchers will conduct an experiment to compare the effectiveness of two brands of shampoo—Brand X and Brand Y.
They will use a matched-pairs design in which each participant will use one brand for one week and then the other brand
for one week. For each participant, the researchers will randomly assign which shampoo, Brand X or Brand Y, will be
used first, and 15 milliliters of shampoo will be used for each hair wash. Because the temperature of the water is known
to have an effect, a water temperature less than 100◦F will be used for the first week and a water temperature greater than
100◦F will be used for the second week. At the end of each week, the effectiveness of the shampoo being used will be
measured.
Which of the following would improve this matched-pairs experiment?

options.A: Have each participant use both brands, but have all participants use Brand X first, then switch to Brand Y
options.B: Have each participant use only one of the shampoo brands, with brand randomly assigned for each participant
options.C: Have each participant use only one of the shampoo brands, with brand determined by the subject’s preference
options.D: Let each participant decide for themselves how much shampoo to use
options.E:
Let each participant decide for themselves what water temperature to use, but each participant must keep the water
temperature the same for both brands
18

text:
Gwyneth measured the weights, in pounds, of ten striped bass taken from a local river. She calculated a mean weight of
13 pounds and a range of 5 pounds. For a report, Gwyneth converted the measurements from pounds to kilograms. (Note:
There is approximately 0.454 kilogram in 1 pound.)
Which of the following is closest to the mean and range of the converted weights?

options.A: The mean weight is 28.63 kilograms, and the range is 5 kilograms.
options.B: The mean weight is 28.63 kilograms, and the range is 2.27 kilograms.
options.C: The mean weight is 5.90 kilograms, and the range is 11.01 kilograms.
options.D: The mean weight is 5.90 kilograms, and the range is 5 kilograms.
options.E: The mean weight is 5.90 kilograms, and the range is 2.27 kilograms.

text:
Karmen and Sophia are on a swim team and race in different events at a swim meet. The probability that Karmen will win
her race at the swim meet is 0.1. The probability that Sophia will win her race at the swim meet is 0.3.
If the events of Karmen winning her race and Sophia winning her race are independent, what is the probability that either
Karmen or Sophia will win her race at the swim meet?

As part of a survey, 118 adults with tattoos were asked whether they got their first tattoo before reaching the age of 25
and whether their first tattoo was done by a professional tattoo artist. The resulting data are summarized in the following
table.
Before the age of 25
25 or older

10

Which of the following provides evidence that age at first tattoo and whether the first tattoo was done by a professional
tattoo artist are not independent for the group of 118 adults with tattoos?

Daniela designed an experiment to investigate whether listening to music or burning a scented candle affects her perfor-
mance while playing her favorite video game. Each day for one month, she randomly chose whether to listen to classical,
rock, or no music while playing her favorite video game. Each day, she also randomly chose whether to burn a candle
with an orange scent, burn a candle with a lavender scent, or not burn a candle. At the end of each day, she recorded the
mean score for 10 plays of her favorite video game.
How many explanatory variables are in the experiment?

options.A: One
options.B: Two
options.C: Three
options.D: Six
options.E: Nine

text:
A real estate agent indicates that the mean selling price of all single-family homes in Washington, D.C., is $800,000.
A family believes the mean selling price for all single-family homes that are currently for sale is less than the amount
indicated by the real estate agent and will select a random sample of single-family homes that are currently for sale in
Washington, D.C. Let µ represent the mean selling price, in dollars, of all single-family homes that are currently for sale
in Washington, D.C.
Assuming that the conditions for inference are met, which of the following hypotheses would be appropriate to test the
family’s belief?

options.E:
H0 : ¯x = 800,000 versus Ha : ¯x < 800,000
END OF SECTION I
20

text:
A large city is considering increasing the number of buses running on a certain route. To determine whether there is a
need for more buses, a random sample of 58 people at bus stops along the route is selected. The amount of time a person
waits until a bus arrives is recorded to the nearest tenth of a minute for each person. The histogram shows the distribution
of wait time. The first bar includes values from 0 to 1.9 minutes, the second bar includes values from 2 to 3.9 minutes,
and so on.
(a) Based on the distribution of wait time shown in the histogram, what would be a statistical advantage of using the
sample median rather than the sample mean as the estimate of the typical wait time?
(b) Using the histogram, describe an appropriate procedure for estimating the sample median wait time. Use the proce-
dure to provide an estimate for the sample median wait time.
(c) Based on the histogram, the smallest possible interquartile range (IQR) for the wait times is 4.1 minutes and the
largest possible IQR for the wait times is 7.9 minutes. Which of these two IQR values would identify more wait
times as potential outliers? Justify your response.
22

Orthodontists use Temporary Anchoring Devices (TADs) to keep teeth in place. TADs can be made of stainless steel or
titanium. A certain amount of damage is expected during use of the TADs, but too much damage to the TADs could result
in a failure to achieve the desired results. An experiment was performed to compare the mean amount of damage to TADs
made of stainless steel to the mean amount of damage to TADs made of titanium. Researchers recruited 386 volunteer
orthodontic patients requiring TADs to participate in the experiment. Each volunteer had a TAD made of stainless steel
placed on one side of the mouth and a TAD made of titanium placed on the other side of the mouth. For each volunteer,
a flip of a fair coin determined which side of the mouth, left (Heads) or right (Tails), received the TAD made of stainless
steel. The other side of the mouth received the TAD made of titanium. After six months, an orthodontist recorded the
amount of damage observed on each TAD on a scale of 1 to 10, with 1 representing the least damage and 10 representing
the most damage.
(a) Identify the experimental units and treatments in the context of this experiment.
• Experimental units
• Treatments
(b) Consider the design of this experiment.
i. Identify the experimental design.
ii. Characteristics of each volunteer, such as age, diet, and dental hygiene, influence the amount of damage to a
TAD. Explain how the design of this experiment controls for the effects of these characteristics.
(c) What would it mean for this experiment to be single-blind?
(d) Suppose the experiment showed no significant difference in the mean amount of damage between the two types of
TADs. Can these results be generalized to all orthodontic patients requiring TADs? Justify your response.
23

Jacob is taking a quiz that consists of a list of five quotations from history and a list of names of the five historical figures
who said them. The quiz directions are to match each quotation on the first list with one of the names on the second list,
matching each name to exactly one quotation.
(a) Consider just the first two quotations. If Jacob randomly guesses two different names from the list of five names for
the first two quotations, what is the probability that he correctly matches both?
Now consider all five quotations and suppose that Jacob randomly guesses the matching name for each quotation,
matching each name to exactly one quotation. Let the random variable X represent the number of correct matches
out of the five quotations. The table shows the probability distribution of X.
Number of correct matches
0
1
2
3
4
5

44
120
45
120
20
120
10
120

120

(b) Calculate the conditional probability that Jacob will match exactly three quotations correctly, given that he matches
at least two of the five quotations correctly. Show your work.
(c) The expected value of the probability distribution of X is 1. What is the interpretation of this expected value in
context?
(d) Does the random variable X have a binomial distribution? Explain why or why not.
24

A large airport has parking spaces spread among 4 types of parking lots. The cost to park a car per day differs from lot to
lot depending on the time it takes to get to the airport terminal. The parking lots are described as follows.
• Premium parking is less than a 5-minute walk to the airport terminal. Premium parking spaces account for 15 percent
of the total number of airport parking spaces.
• General parking is a 5- to 10-minute walk to the airport terminal. General parking spaces account for 29.5 percent
of the total number of airport parking spaces.
• Economy parking is a 10- to 15-minute walk to the airport terminal. Economy parking spaces account for 37 percent
of the total number of airport parking spaces.
• Off-site parking uses a shuttle that operates every 15 minutes to transport people from the off-site parking lot to the
airport terminal. Off-site parking spaces account for 18.5 percent of the total number of airport parking spaces.
Tens of thousands of drivers park in the airport’s parking lots each year. A random sample of 343 drivers who park in
the airport’s parking lots is selected during a given year, and each driver is asked where they would choose to park if all
parking lot options were available to them. Airport officials counted the number of responses for each type of parking lot,
and the results are summarized in the table.
Type of Parking Lot
Number of Responses
Premium
63
General
125
Economy
111
Off-site
44
Total
343
Do the data in the table provide convincing statistical evidence that at least one of the proportions of all drivers who park
in the airport’s parking lots who would choose each type of parking lot is different from the proportion of the total number
of airport parking spaces accounted for by that type of parking lot? Complete the appropriate inference procedure to
support your answer using a significance level of α = 0.05.

An important economic indicator is the labor participation rate. The labor participation rate is the proportion of the

Solution
Part (a):
A statistical advantage of using the median is that the median will better represent the center of the bus wait times. The
histogram of the wait times is skewed towards higher values. Because the distribution is skewed to the right and
potentially has outliers, it is better to use the median, as the mean is pulled towards the skew of the graph. If a distribution
is skewed right, then the mean is usually to the right of the median. The median is resistant to outliers because outliers do
not greatly, if at all, affect the value of the median, but the mean is influenced by outliers. The median will likely coincide
with the peak of the graph, representing the typical wait time, while the mean will likely be to the right of the peak, not
representing the typical wait time.
Scoring
Part (a) is scored as follows:
Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following four components:
Describes how skewness (or outliers) does not affect the median
Describes how skewness (or outliers) does affect the mean
Indicates the median will better represent the center of the distribution
Includes context
Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies only two or three of the four components.
Incorrect (I) if the response does not satisfy the criteria for E or P.

add the number of passengers in each wait time bin until you reach the 29th and 30th passengers,

Additional Notes:
• To satisfy component 1, the response may indicate that the experimental units are the patients. The response
could refer to patients, participants, subjects, or volunteers without explicitly mentioning the number 386.
• To satisfy component 2, identification of the treatments must indicate that there are exactly two types of TADs.
• A response that describes the treatment groups rather than the treatment, e.g., the right side of the mouth receives
the stainless steel TAD and the left side of the mouth receives the titanium TAD or the left side of the mouth
receives the titanium TAD and the right side of the mouth receives the stainless steel TAD, may satisfy
component 2.
• A response that describes the placement of the TAD, e.g., which side of the mouth receives the stainless steel
TAD and which side receives the titanium TAD, does not satisfy component 2.
• If multiple answers are provided, score the weakest answer. For example, if a response says that the experimental
units are “the 386 volunteers and the damage result from 0 to 10,” component 1 is not satisfied.
Solution
Part (b):
(i) This is a matched pairs experiment.
(ii) A matched pairs experiment will allow for control of the volunteer characteristics because each volunteer receives
both TADs: stainless steel and titanium. Because both types of TADs are given to the same person, the environment the
pair of TADs undergoes during the study period is the same.
Scoring
Part (b) is scored as follows:
Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following two components:
Indicates a matched-pair experiment
Indicates that the characteristics listed (or any other reasonable confounding variable) are controlled by using the TADs as
pairs in the same volunteer
Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies only one of the two components.
Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.
Additional Notes:
• A response that indicates the design is a randomized block design and identifies each patient as a block, may
satisfy component 1.
• If a response does not satisfy component 1, the response may satisfy component 2 with a correct explanation of
how a matched pairs design controls the effects of confounding variables.

variables without giving an example does not satisfy component 2.

Solution
Part (c):
In a single-blind study either the volunteer or the researcher (but not both) is unaware which treatment is being used. In
this case, it is likely that the patient would not know which side of the mouth had the titanium TAD and which side had
the stainless steel TAD, but the orthodontist would know by the look or shape of the TAD.
Scoring
Part (c) is scored as follows:
Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following three components:
States that the participants are blind to the treatment assignment
States that the orthodontist is not blind to the treatment assignment
Includes context
Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies only two of the three components.
Incorrect (I) if the response does not satisfy the criteria for E or P.
Additional Notes:
• If the response uses “group” instead of “orthodontist” or “volunteer” in components 1 or 2, the response is scored
at most P.
• If the response states that one of the treatment groups can distinguish between the stainless steel TADs and the
titanium TADs and the other treatment group cannot distinguish between the TADs, the response is scored at
most P.
• A response that states that the orthodontist (or researcher) is blinded, but the participants are not blinded may
satisfy components 1 and 2.
• A description of how the look or feel of the TAD may be different is not required to satisfy any component.
• A response that states the patient or the orthodontists cannot distinguish between stainless steel TADs and
titanium TADs may satisfy only component 1.
Solution
Part (d):
Because the TADs were randomly assigned to the side of the mouth, the results of the study could be generalized to all
patients similar to those in the study. However, the patients in the study are volunteers and may not be representative of all
orthodontic patients requiring TADs. Therefore, the results of the study CANNOT be generalized to all orthodontic
patients requiring TADs.

Based on the original scatterplot, there appears to be a fairly strong, positive association between the age and length of the
turtles.

boxplots show the median for male ages is higher (older) than about 75% of the females and the point-biserial correlation
between sex and age is 0.50.
In this case, age would be a confounding variable because age is associated with the sex of the turtles and influences the
length of the turtles. Therefore, it can’t be determined if the differences in the lengths of the turtles are due to their age or
their sex, or a combination of both.

States that age appears to be a confounding variable in the association between the length and sex of the turtles

Indicates that there is an association between the age and sex of the turtles
