A machine at a large factory fills bottles with juice. To reduce the likelihood of bottles containing less than 8 ounces (oz) of juice, the machine is set to fill the bottles with a mean of 8.05 oz of juice. To investigate whether the machine is working properly, the factory manager randomly samples ten bottles every 24 hours and records data for several variables from each of the sampled bottles.

Which of the following is classified as a continuous quantitative variable for the factory manager’s investigation?

options.A: Whether a randomly chosen bottle contains less than 8 oz of juice
options.B: Whether all of the bottles in the sample contain less than 8 oz of juice
options.C: The number of bottles that contain less than 8 oz of juice out of a random sample of 10 bottles of juice
options.D: The number of properly filled bottles of juice at a randomly selected time
options.E: The amount of juice, in ounces, in a randomly selected bottle

text:
Chicken eggs are categorized into sizes based on weight. The table displays the sizes and associated weights, in grams (g), for chicken eggs sold in a certain country.

Egg Size and Weight

| Size       | Small        | Medium       | Large        | Extra Large  |
|------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| Weight     | Less than 53 g | 53 g to less than 63 g | 63 g to less than 73 g | 73 g or more |

Weights of chicken eggs sold in the country are normally distributed with mean $\mu = 62 \text{g}$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 6 \text{g}$.

Which of the following is closest to the probability that a randomly selected chicken egg sold in the country is either small or extra large?

The following boxplot summarizes the annual snowfall amounts, in inches, for a sample of 25 cities in the United States.

Snowfall Amount

       0   10   20   30   40   50   60
Annual Snowfall Amount (inches)

Based on the boxplot, which of the following can be concluded?

options.A: The first quartile is about 20 inches.
options.B: The median is about 20 inches.
options.C: The third quartile is about 30 inches.
options.D: The interquartile range is about 20 inches.
options.E: The range is about 40 inches.

text:
According to a survey, 32 percent of people in a certain country regularly recycle. Mr. Giovanni, the mayor of a specific city in the country, wanted to know whether the residents in his city regularly recycle more than the national average. He selected a random sample of 50 residents, 28 of whom regularly recycle. Mr. Giovanni would like to use a one-sample z-test for a population proportion to test the hypotheses $H_0: p = 0.32$ versus $H_a: p > 0.32$, where $p$ is the proportion of city residents who regularly recycle. In order to use this test, the sampling distribution of the sample proportion needs to be approximately normal.

Which of the following is true for Mr. Giovanni's study and ensures that the sampling distribution of the sample proportion is approximately normal?

options.A: The distribution of the population of all residents who regularly recycle is approximately normal.
options.B: The sample size is 50, which is larger than 30.
options.C: The sample size of 50 is less than 10% of the residents in the city.
options.D: The expected number of residents who regularly recycle is 16, and the expected number of residents who do not regularly recycle is 34, and both numbers are at least 10.
options.E: The observed number of residents who regularly recycle is 28, and the observed number of residents who do not regularly recycle is 22, and both numbers are at least 10.

text:
A group of farmers wants to estimate the mean length of carrots grown on their farms. The farmers will select a random sample of carrots from the farms and will measure each carrot's length, in inches, and compute the mean and standard deviation of the sample.

Assuming the conditions for inference are met, which of the following is the most appropriate method to use for such an estimate?

options.A: A one-sample $t$-interval for a population mean
options.B: A one-sample $t$-interval for a sample mean
options.C: A one-sample $z$-interval for a population mean
options.D: A one-sample $z$-interval for a population proportion
options.E: A one-sample $z$-interval for a sample proportion

text:
Scores from a robotics competition have a mean of 19.9 points and a standard deviation of 7.2 points. Scores from an art competition have a mean of 20.2 points and a standard deviation of 5.7 points. Dominic enters both competitions and gets a score of 27 in robotics and a score of 26 in art.

Based on Dominic’s standardized scores, in which competition did Dominic have a better performance?

options.A: Robotics, because Dominic’s standardized score in robotics is approximately 0.99, while his standardized score in art is approximately 0.85.
options.B: Robotics, because Dominic’s standardized score in robotics is approximately 1.19, while his standardized score in art is approximately 1.02.
options.C: Art, because Dominic’s standardized score in art is approximately 1.19, while his standardized score in robotics is approximately 0.85.
options.D: Art, because Dominic’s standardized score in art is approximately 1.02, while his standardized score in robotics is approximately 0.99.
options.E: Art, because Dominic’s standardized score in art is approximately 0.99, while his standardized score in robotics is approximately 1.02.

text:
A professional sports league has 30 teams. A fan of one of the teams is interested in the mean yearly salary of players on that team. The fan obtains the yearly salary of each player for all 28 players on the team.

Which of the following best describes the fan’s sampling plan?

options.A: Simple random sample
options.B: Stratified random sample, with teams as strata
options.C: Cluster sample, with teams as clusters
options.D: Systematic random sample
options.E: Census

text: In an experiment, Jaden, a statistics student, wants to investigate whether listening to music while playing a game of chess results in a game with more total moves. Jaden's large college has 200 students who play chess and are willing to participate in the experiment. He allows the players to decide whether they will play a chess game against a computer program while listening to music or will play a chess game against the same computer program while not listening to music. The total number of moves it takes for each chess game will be recorded. Which of the following statements best describes why this is not a well-designed experiment?

options.A: The subjects cannot be blind to the treatment because they will know whether they are listening to music.
options.B: The treatments were not randomly assigned.
options.C: There is not a control group of players who do not play chess but do listen to music.
options.D: There was no replication because each player plays only one game against the computer program.
options.E: The players were not randomly selected.

text:
In a certain game, a player wins a prize by randomly picking a red marble from a bag of differently colored marbles. The probability that a player will win a prize on a given play of the game is 0.26.

Assuming successive plays of the game are independent, which of the following statements is true?

options.A: If a player plays this game a very large number of times, the player will win a prize about 26% of the time.
options.B: If a player plays the game 100 times, the player will win a prize exactly 26 times.
options.C: If a player does not win a prize in 25 consecutive plays, the player is guaranteed to win a prize on the 26th play.
options.D: It is impossible for the player to win a prize on two consecutive plays of the game.
options.E: The player is guaranteed to win exactly one prize in 26 consecutive plays of the game.

text:
A simple random sample of $n = 23$ people who commute from home to work in a large metropolitan area is selected. Each person is asked their distance, in miles, from home to work and how long it typically takes them, in minutes, to commute from home to work. The distance, $x$, and typical commute time, $y$, are recorded for each person in the sample. A least-squares regression model using distance to predict typical commute time results in the following computer output.

Regression Output

| Predictor | Coefficient | SE of Coefficient | T | P-Value |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Constant | 5.514 | 1.805 | 3.06 | 0.0063 |
| Distance | 1.216 | 0.103 | 11.86 | < 0.0001 |
| $S = 5.610$ | $r^2 = 0.875$ |  |  |  |

Assuming all conditions for inference are met, which of the following is a 90 percent confidence interval for the predicted change in typical commute time for a one-mile increase in the distance from home to work?

Blue light is the wavelength of light given off by electronic devices and energy-efficient lightbulbs. Researchers are interested in investigating the effect of blue light on sleep. The researchers conduct a study using 30 volunteers. There are two light settings used in the study: one with a high amount of blue light and one with a low amount of blue light. Each volunteer will spend the night in the researchers' sleep lab on two different nights. On one of the nights, each volunteer will be exposed to one of the two light settings for 50 minutes prior to their usual bedtime. On another night, each volunteer will be exposed to the other light setting for 50 minutes prior to their usual bedtime. The light setting that is experienced first is randomized for each volunteer. After each night in the sleep lab, the amount of melatonin, a hormone associated with the sleep-wake cycle, is measured for each volunteer. When measuring the results, the researchers know which light setting each volunteer was exposed to each night.

Which experimental design or method are the researchers using for the study?

options.A: Double-blind experiment
options.B: Randomized complete block design, blocked by usual bedtime
options.C: Completely randomized design
options.D: Matched pairs design
options.E: This is an observational study, not an experiment.

text:
A researcher in a certain country claims that the proportion of people in their twenties (people aged 20 to 29) who can lift more than 200 pounds is greater than the proportion of people in their forties (people aged 40 to 49) who can lift more than 200 pounds. In a random sample of people in their twenties living in that country, 31 percent could lift more than 200 pounds. In a separate random sample of people in their forties living in that country, 22 percent could lift more than 200 pounds.

Let $p_1$ be the proportion of all people in their twenties living in the country who can lift more than 200 pounds. Let $p_2$ be the proportion of all people in their forties living in the country who can lift more than 200 pounds.

Which of the following are the appropriate null and alternative hypotheses for testing the researcher’s claim for these two age-groups?

Many cities are hiring artists to create asphalt art, in which artists paint murals directly on certain roads in hopes of getting people to slow down and follow the speed limit along those roads. During a 24-hour period, a researcher measured the speeds of all drivers who drove on randomly selected roads. Some roads had art, and some roads did not have art. Each road had a speed limit of 35 miles per hour (mph). The distributions of speed for both types of roads are displayed in the following boxplots.

Speed on Roads

Based on the boxplots, which of the following is a true statement?

options.A: Because the speed of the first quartile on the roads with art is less than the minimum speed on the roads without art, it is not likely that drivers on roads with art travel at slower speeds.
options.B: Because the range of the speeds for roads with art and the range of the speeds for roads without art are each approximately 20 mph, it is likely that drivers on roads with art do not travel at slower speeds.
options.C: Because the interquartile range of the speeds on the roads with art is less than the interquartile range of the speeds on the roads without art, it is likely that drivers on roads with art travel at slower speeds.
options.D: Because the maximum speed on the roads with art is greater than the median speed on the roads without art, it is likely that drivers on roads with art travel at slower speeds.
options.E: Because each value of the five-number summary for the roads with art is less than the corresponding value of the five-number summary for the roads without art, it is likely that drivers on roads with art travel at slower speeds.

text:
Spam e-mail (unwanted electronic mail) is usually sent in bulk to a large list of recipients. A large e-mail
service provider estimates that 4 percent of all e-mails are spam.

In a random sample of 50 e-mails processed by the service provider, which of the following is the approximate
probability that the sample will have at least 3 spam e-mails?

A company owns a group of 50 cars, all the same make, model, and year. The group has a mean fuel economy
μ = 23.2 miles per gallon (mpg) and a standard deviation σ = 2.4 mpg. A simple random sample of 4 cars
from the group will be selected.

What are the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean fuel economy for
samples of size 4?

options.A: Mean = 23.2 mpg, standard deviation = 2.4 mpg
options.B: Mean = 23.2 mpg, standard deviation = 1.2 mpg
options.C: Mean = 23.2 mpg, standard deviation = 0.6 mpg
options.D: Mean = 11.6 mpg, standard deviation = 2.4 mpg
options.E: Mean = 11.6 mpg, standard deviation = 1.2 mpg

text:
A factory that manufactures glass has two shifts of workers: a daytime shift and an evening shift. A random sample of 200 pieces of glass was selected from each shift, and the pieces were carefully inspected for defects. From the daytime shift, 12 pieces of glass were defective. From the evening shift, 15 pieces of glass were defective.

Assuming all conditions for inference are met, which of the following gives a 98 percent confidence interval for the difference (daytime shift minus evening shift) in the population proportion of defective glass for the two shifts?

Information obtained from a random sample of 40 entry-level lawyers working in a certain country was used to calculate a 95 percent confidence interval for the true mean salary of all entry-level lawyers in the country. The confidence interval is ($56,100, $58,900).

Assume the conditions for inference are met. Which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: Because the interval includes the value of $58,000, the confidence interval provides convincing statistical evidence at the α = 0.05 level of significance that the average salary for entry-level lawyers in the country is $58,000.
options.B: Because the interval includes the value of $58,000, the confidence interval does not provide convincing statistical evidence at the α = 0.05 level of significance that the average salary for entry-level lawyers in the country is different from $58,000.
options.C: Because the center of the confidence interval is $57,500, there is convincing statistical evidence at the α = 0.05 level of significance that the average salary for entry-level lawyers in the country is $57,500.
options.D: Because all of the values in the interval are less than $60,000, the confidence interval does not provide convincing statistical evidence at the α = 0.05 level of significance that the average salary for entry-level lawyers in the country is different from $60,000.
options.E: Because the interval begins at $56,100, no lawyer in the sample had a salary less than that amount.

text:
A report was published on the number of children under the age of 18 per household in a certain country. A researcher for a city in that country wants to determine whether the distribution of the number of children per household in the city is different from that of the country as a whole. The researcher selected a random sample of 200 households from the city and obtained information on the number of children in each household.

The table displays the percentage of households in the country with the corresponding number of children. The observed number of households with the corresponding number of children from the random sample of 200 households in the city is also shown.

Number of Children

| Number of children | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 or more |
|--------------------|---|---|---|---|-----------|
| Percentage of all the country's households | 20% | 39% | 25% | 10% | 6% |
| Observed number of households | 24 | 68 | 66 | 20 | 22 |

Assuming all conditions for inference are met, which of the following is an appropriate hypothesis test for the researcher to use?

options.A: One-sample z-test for a population proportion
options.B: Two-sample z-test for a difference in population proportions
options.C: Chi-square test for goodness of fit
options.D: Chi-square test for homogeneity
options.E: Chi-square test for independence

text:
A researcher at a car manufacturing company was interested in knowing whether the type of car a driver uses the most (gasoline, hybrid, or electric) varied from Country A to Country B. The researcher obtained a random sample of 1,000 car owners living in Country A and a separate random sample of 2,000 car owners living in Country B. The following table lists the frequencies for the type of car the driver uses the most among the car owners who were sampled from each country.

Type of Car Most Used for Each Country

|          | Gasoline | Hybrid | Electric | Total |
|----------|----------|--------|----------|-------|
| Country A | 647      | 203    | 150      | 1,000 |
| Country B | 554      | 1,294  | 152      | 2,000 |
| Total     | 1,201    | 1,497  | 302      | 3,000 |

Based on the data in the table, which of the following statements is true?

options.A: The proportion of hybrid or electric cars in Country A's sample is more than the proportion of hybrid or electric cars in Country B's sample.
options.B: The proportion of gasoline cars in Country A's sample is less than the proportion of electric cars in Country B's sample.
options.C: The proportion of gasoline cars in Country A's sample is equal to the proportion of hybrid cars in Country B's sample.
options.D: The proportion of electric cars is the same for both samples.
options.E: The proportion of gasoline cars is the highest in both samples.

text:
The owner of a large peach orchard has both genetically modified (GM) peach trees and traditional peach trees in the orchard. The orchard has hundreds of each type of peach tree. The owner believes that on average the GM peach trees produce heavier peaches than the traditional peach trees. To test this belief, the owner randomly sampled 50 GM peaches and 50 traditional peaches and weighed each peach. The owner conducted a two-sample t-test for the difference in the population mean weight of peaches for the two types of peach trees. The p-value of the test is 0.12.

Which of the following is an appropriate conclusion for the owner to make at a 5% level of significance?

options.A: The owner fails to reject the null hypothesis and does not have convincing statistical evidence that the GM trees produce heavier peaches, on average, compared to the traditional trees.
options.B: The owner fails to reject the null hypothesis and has convincing statistical evidence that the GM trees produce heavier peaches, on average, compared to the traditional trees.
options.C: The owner rejects the null hypothesis and does not have convincing statistical evidence that the GM trees produce heavier peaches, on average, compared to the traditional trees.
options.D: The owner rejects the null hypothesis and has convincing statistical evidence that the GM trees produce heavier peaches, on average, compared to the traditional trees.
options.E: The owner should not make a conclusion because the sample size is not large enough to satisfy the normality condition for the test.

text:
A number cube has six sides, each with equal probability of landing face-up when rolled, which is also known as a fair number cube. A statistics teacher is trying to create a six-sided number cube where each side does not have an equal probability of landing face-up when rolled. A claim is made that after baking a fair number cube for 10 minutes at 175 degrees Fahrenheit (°F), each face will no longer have an equal probability of landing face-up when the number cube is rolled. Thus, the distribution of the number landing face-up will not be uniform. To test this claim, the statistics teacher baked a fair number cube for 10 minutes at 175°F and recorded the results of 200 number cube rolls, shown in the following table.

Results from 200 Rolls of Baked Number Cube

| Number Landing Face-Up | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|------------------------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency              | 24 | 34 | 32 | 31 | 33 | 46 |

All conditions for inference were met, and the test statistic and p-value for the appropriate hypothesis test were 7.66 and 0.176, respectively.

Which of the following statements is the best interpretation of the p-value?

options.A: The probability is 0.176 that the distribution of the number landing face-up is uniform after the number cube is baked.
options.B: The probability is 0.176 that the distribution of the number landing face-up is not uniform after the number cube is baked.
options.C: The probability of observing the distribution of the number landing face-up in the table is 0.176.
options.D: If the distribution of the number landing face-up is uniform, the probability of observing a test statistic of at least 7.66 is 0.176.
options.E: If the distribution of the number landing face-up is uniform, the probability of observing a test statistic of at most 7.66 is 0.176.

text:
A large regional sports league wants all teams to use the same airline company when they travel on Fridays to a particular city for games. The league wants to ensure the teams arrive on time as often as possible. The league records all arrivals (both on-time and late) for six airline companies on all Fridays for the past year to that city. The bar chart displays the number of on-time arrivals for the six companies.

Airline On-Time Arrivals

Frequency

Airline Company

Based on the bar chart, which airline company should the league select so that the teams are more likely to arrive on time?

options.A: Airline 6, because Airline 6 has the largest number of on-time arrivals.
options.B: Airline 2, because Airline 2 has the fewest flights, so it is more likely that the airline can arrive on time.
options.C: A decision cannot be made because this is categorical data. The airlines could have been presented in any order on the bar chart; thus the data will be skewed.
options.D: A decision cannot be made based on frequency. A comparison of the relative frequency of on-time arrivals for each of the six airline companies is needed.
options.E: A decision cannot be made because the data are based on a census. A random sample of Fridays would be required.

text:
At a grocery store, there are three self-checkout stations for customers to complete their purchases. Let $X = $ the number of self-checkout stations being used by customers at any given time. The probability distribution of $X$ is given in the following table.

Probability Distribution of Self-Checkout Station Use

| x | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P(X = x)$ | 0.08 | 0.22 | 0.32 | 0.38 |

The mean of $X$ is 2. Which of the following is closest to the standard deviation of $X$?

There is a certain compound found in the cacao plant that can affect blood pressure. A researcher is studying whether adding a capsule that contains this compound along with regular medication will aid in reducing blood pressure. The researcher recruited 22 volunteer patients who had approximately the same blood pressure and were taking the same brand of blood pressure medicine. The researcher randomly assigned 11 of the volunteers to take the compound capsule along with their current medication and the other 11 of the volunteers to take a placebo capsule along with their current medication. After one month, the systolic blood pressure, in millimeters of mercury (mmHg), was taken for the volunteers and recorded. The table displays summary statistics for the systolic blood pressure for each group of volunteers.

Summary Statistics for Systolic Blood Pressure

| | Number of Volunteers | Mean (mmHg) | Standard Deviation (mmHg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compound capsule | 11 | 118.6 | 5.35 |
| Placebo capsule | 11 | 123.5 | 6.53 |

Assuming all conditions for inference are met, which of the following is a 95 percent confidence interval for the difference between the means of the systolic blood pressure (compound minus placebo), in mmHg, for all patients similar to those in the study?

A strong, heat-resistant synthetic fiber currently used in manufacturing has a mean strength of 1.62 gigapascals (GPa). Scientists believe that spider silk, a natural material, is stronger on average than the currently used fiber. A scientist randomly selected 10 strands of spider silk and determined the strength of each strand. The sample mean strength was 1.87 GPa with a sample standard deviation of 0.26 GPa. The scientist conducted an appropriate test of significance for the following hypotheses.

H₀: μₛᵢₗₖ = 1.62

Hₐ: μₛᵢₗₖ > 1.62

Assuming all conditions for inference are met, what are the values of the appropriate test statistic and associated p-value?

Farmer Jones and Farmer O'Grady grow avocados in two different states. The farmers want to estimate the difference in the proportion of avocados from their large farms that cannot be sold because they are rotten.

Which of the following combinations of sample sizes and proportions of rotten avocados for each of the two farms would not allow the farmers to assume the sampling distribution of the difference in sample proportions of rotten avocados will have an approximately normal distribution?

options.A: Farmer Jones takes a sample of size 100 and observes that the proportion of avocados that are rotten is 0.40. Farmer O'Grady takes a sample of size 50 and observes that the proportion of avocados that are rotten is 0.20.
options.B: Farmer Jones takes a sample of size 100 and observes that the proportion of avocados that are rotten is 0.12. Farmer O'Grady takes a sample of size 50 and observes that the proportion of avocados that are rotten is 0.06.
options.C: Farmer Jones takes a sample of size 200 and observes that the proportion of avocados that are rotten is 0.40. Farmer O'Grady takes a sample of size 100 and observes that the proportion of avocados that are rotten is 0.20.
options.D: Farmer Jones takes a sample of size 200 and observes that the proportion of avocados that are rotten is 0.06. Farmer O'Grady takes a sample of size 200 and observes that the proportion of avocados that are rotten is 0.06.
options.E: Farmer Jones takes a sample of size 300 and observes that the proportion of avocados that are rotten is 0.06. Farmer O'Grady takes a sample of size 50 and observes that the proportion of avocados that are rotten is 0.20.

text:
A wildlife sanctuary is a place where wild animals are kept safe. A researcher at a certain sanctuary is interested in the relationship between weight and average daily amount of water consumed by lions in the sanctuary. To investigate this relationship, the researcher selected a random sample of 20 lions from the sanctuary. The weight, in pounds, and the average daily amount of water consumed, in ounces, was recorded for each lion for a period of 5 days. From the data, a least-squares regression model was constructed using weight as the explanatory variable and average daily amount of water consumed as the response variable. A computer summary of the least-squares regression model is shown in the following table.

Regression Output

| Predictor | Coefficient | SE of Coefficient | T | p-Value |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Constant | 32.239 | 12.781 | 2.522 | 0.021 |
| Weight | 0.039 | 0.093 | 0.419 | 0.680 |
| S = 13.589 | r² = 0.010 |  |  |  |

The researcher will use the regression results to test the hypotheses H₀: There is not a linear relationship between weight and average daily amount of water consumed by all lions in the sanctuary versus Hₐ: There is a linear relationship between weight and average daily amount of water consumed by all lions in the sanctuary. Assuming the conditions for inference were met, which of the following statements is true at the 0.05 significance level?

options.A: Because the r²-value of 0.010 is less than α = 0.05, the data provide convincing statistical evidence of a linear relationship between weight and average daily amount of water consumed for all lions in the sanctuary.
options.B: Because the r²-value of 0.010 is less than α = 0.05, the data provide convincing statistical evidence of a linear relationship between weight and average daily amount of water consumed for the 20 lions in the study.
options.C: Because the p-value of 0.680 is greater than α = 0.05, the data provide convincing statistical evidence of a linear relationship between weight and average daily amount of water consumed for all lions in the sanctuary.
options.D: Because the p-value of 0.680 is greater than α = 0.05, the data do not provide convincing statistical evidence of a linear relationship between weight and average daily amount of water consumed for all lions in the sanctuary.
options.E: Because the p-value of 0.680 is greater than α = 0.05, the data do not provide convincing statistical evidence of a linear relationship between weight and average daily amount of water consumed for the 20 lions in the study.

text:
According to an association of dentists, half of all adults over the age of 30 suffer from some form of gum disease. One indicator of the health of gums is the gap between the pocket of the gum and the nearby tooth, measured in millimeters (mm). These gap measurements range from 0 to 7 mm, with 0 indicating no gum disease and 7 indicating the most severe gum disease.

A group of dentists claims that improving tooth-brushing techniques will decrease the greatest gap measurement, on average, of patients in their large practice. The dentists randomly select 32 patients from the approximately 1,500 patients in their dental practice and measure the greatest gap at the beginning and then again at the end of a three-year period of improved tooth-brushing techniques. Summary statistics of the measurements are displayed in the table.

Summary Statistics for Greatest Gap Measurements

|  | n | Mean (mm) | Standard Deviation (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Greatest gap at beginning of study | 32 | 3.7 | 0.62 |
| Greatest gap at end of study | 32 | 3.5 | 0.58 |
| Difference (beginning minus end) | 32 | 0.2 | 0.40 |

Assuming all conditions for inference are met, which of the following is the correct value of the test statistic and p-value to test the dentists’ claim?

options.A: Test statistic = 2.828, p-value = 0.0041
options.B: Test statistic = 2.828, p-value = 0.0081
options.C: Test statistic = 1.333, p-value = 0.0193
options.D: Test statistic = 1.333, p-value = 0.0938
options.E: Test statistic = 1.333, p-value = 0.1875

text:
An aerial view of a large suburban area shows that 40 percent of the residences have gardens and 30 percent of the residences have solar panels. Among the residences with gardens, 50 percent have solar panels.

If a residence is selected at random, what is the probability that the residence has a garden, solar panels, or both?

According to a recent survey, 40 percent of all households in a certain country have newspapers delivered to their homes. Holly, a local city manager, is investigating whether the percentage of households in her city that have newspapers delivered is different from the national percentage reported in the survey. The hypotheses for her investigation are H₀: p = 0.40 versus Hₐ: p ≠ 0.40, where p is the proportion of all households in the city that have newspapers delivered. To test these hypotheses at the significance level of α = 0.05, Holly selected a simple random sample of 85 households. Among the households sampled, 42 reported having newspapers delivered.

Assuming the conditions for inference are met, which of the following is the correct inference decision?

options.A: The p-value is larger than the significance level, so there is convincing statistical evidence that the proportion of city households that have newspapers delivered is equal to 0.40.
options.B: The p-value is larger than the significance level, so there is convincing statistical evidence that the proportion of city households that have newspapers delivered is different from 0.40.
options.C: The p-value is larger than the significance level, so there is not convincing statistical evidence that the proportion of city households that have newspapers delivered is different from 0.40.
options.D: The p-value is smaller than the significance level, so there is convincing statistical evidence that the proportion of city households that have newspapers delivered is equal to 0.40.
options.E: The p-value is smaller than the significance level, so there is convincing statistical evidence that the proportion of city households that have newspapers delivered is different from 0.40.

text:
Ms. Sato, a president of a large university, is interested in estimating the proportion of students from the university who are fluent in more than one language. She takes a random sample of 2,200 students from the university. The 95 percent confidence interval she calculates to estimate the population proportion of students from the university who are fluent in more than one language is (0.274, 0.312).

Assuming all conditions for inference have been met, which of the following is the best interpretation of the 95 percent confidence interval?

options.A: Because zero is not contained within the confidence interval, it can be assumed that there is a difference in the proportion of students from the university who are fluent in more than one language.
options.B: The president can be 95% confident that the population proportion of students from the university who are fluent in more than one language is 0.293.
options.C: The president is confident that, in repeated sampling, 95% of the time, the sample proportion of students from the university who are fluent in more than one language will be 0.293.
options.D: The president is 95% confident because 95% of all samples will produce a confidence interval for the population proportion of students from the university who are fluent in more than one language between 0.274 and 0.312.
options.E: The president can be 95% confident that the population proportion of students from the university who are fluent in more than one language is between 0.274 and 0.312.

text:
A study was conducted to investigate the association between temperature and length of the wings of butterflies. A random sample of butterflies of a certain species was collected across a continent. On the day each butterfly was collected, the day’s mean temperature at that location, in degrees Celsius (°C), and the butterfly’s forewing length, in millimeters (mm), were recorded. From these data, a least-squares regression line was calculated to predict forewing length (mm) from mean temperature (°C).

The equation of the least-squares regression line is $\hat{y} = 22.550 + 0.262x$, where $x$ is the mean temperature on the day each butterfly was collected and $y$ is the forewing length of the butterfly.

On the day one butterfly in the sample was collected, the mean temperature was $20.80^\circ\mathrm{C}$ and the forewing length of the butterfly was $27.15\ \mathrm{mm}$.

Using the least-squares regression line, which of the following is closest to the residual for this butterfly?

options.A: $-6.68\ \mathrm{mm}$
options.B: $-0.85\ \mathrm{mm}$
options.C: $0.26\ \mathrm{mm}$
options.D: $0.85\ \mathrm{mm}$
options.E: $28.00\ \mathrm{mm}$

text:
According to a report, only 18 percent of all drivers can drive a car with a manual transmission. Mr. Jerry, a morning radio show host in a certain city, was curious to know whether the proportion of the show's listeners who can drive a car with a manual transmission is different from 0.18. He requested that listeners call the show and report whether they can drive a car with a manual transmission. Mr. Jerry did not have time to talk to everyone who called, so he took a random sample of the callers by rolling a die. Because the die landed on 5, the fifth call was answered and then every fifth call after that one was answered until there were 40 responses from the 200 listeners who called. Of the callers who were selected, 25 percent stated that they can drive a car with a manual transmission.

Which of the following is true about the results of the survey conducted by the radio host?

options.A: The radio host took a random sample of the callers, so the sample proportion of 0.25 may be an accurate estimate of the true proportion of drivers in the city who can drive a car with a manual transmission.
options.B: The radio host took a random sample of the callers, so the sample proportion of 0.25 may be an accurate estimate of the true proportion of morning radio show listeners who can drive a car with a manual transmission.
options.C: The radio host's sample size is more than 30%, so the sample proportion of 0.25 may be an accurate estimate of the true proportion of morning radio show listeners who can drive a car with a manual transmission.
options.D: The radio host's sample contains people who chose to participate, so 0.25 may not be an accurate estimate of the true proportion of morning radio show listeners who can drive a car with a manual transmission.
options.E: Because 40(0.18) = 7.2, which is not greater than 10, 0.25 may not be an accurate estimate of the true proportion of morning radio show listeners who can drive a car with a manual transmission.

text:
In some years at a certain beach, loud concerts are held every weekend during the summer. In other years, there are no concerts held during the summer. A bird lover is concerned that the concerts affect the number of eggs a certain type of bird lays on the beach.

The bird lover took a random sample of years with concerts and another random sample of years without concerts. Using available records, the bird lover recorded the number of eggs birds of the specified type laid on the beach each year. A two-sample t-test for the difference in population means was conducted, and the bird lover concluded the mean number of eggs the birds laid each year is significantly less in the years when there were summer concerts.

Assuming the conditions for inference were met, which of the following is a correct statement about the conclusion that can be made from the study?

options.A: The concerts cause a decrease in the mean number of eggs the birds lay on that certain beach.
options.B: The concerts cause a decrease in the mean number of eggs the birds lay on all beaches with this type of bird.
options.C: The concerts do not cause a decrease in the mean number of eggs the birds lay on that certain beach.
options.D: It cannot be determined whether the concerts cause a decrease in the mean number of eggs the birds lay because this is an observational study.
options.E: It cannot be determined whether the concerts cause a decrease in the mean number of eggs the birds lay because the bird lover does not know whether the loudness of the concerts used each week is constant.

text:
In a certain large city, a recycling bin is considered to be used correctly if it does not contain any food or other biological waste (such as used tissues or diapers). In the city, 65 percent of all households with recycling bins use the bins correctly.

In a random sample of 600 households in the city, what are the mean, $\mu$, and standard deviation, $\sigma$, of the number of households with recycling bins that use them correctly?

A company with many grocery stores buys grapefruit from two regions, X and Y. A produce buyer at the company believes that grapefruit grown in region X weigh more, on average, than grapefruit grown in region Y. The buyer randomly selects and weighs 125 grapefruits from each region. Let μX be the mean weight of all grapefruit from region X and let μY be the mean weight of all grapefruit from region Y. The buyer uses the data to conduct a two-sample t-test to test the following hypotheses.

H₀: μX = μY

Ha: μX > μY

The assumptions for inference are met, and the buyer makes a decision based on the data collected and the results of the inference test.

Which of the following describes a Type II error for this test?

options.A: The buyer rejects the null hypothesis and determines there is convincing statistical evidence that μX > μY, when in fact μX = μY.
options.B: The buyer fails to reject the null hypothesis and determines there is convincing statistical evidence that μX < μY, when in fact μX = μY.
options.C: The buyer fails to reject the null hypothesis and determines there is not convincing statistical evidence that μX > μY, when in fact μX > μY.
options.D: The buyer rejects the null hypothesis and determines there is convincing statistical evidence that μX > μY, when in fact μX > μY.
options.E: The buyer fails to reject the null hypothesis and determines there is not convincing statistical evidence that μX > μY, when in fact μX = μY.

text:
A random sample of 1,092 people were asked whether color was a consideration in buying a new car. They were also asked to identify one additional feature that was important. The responses are shown in the table.

| Color Consideration | Yes | No | Maybe | Total |
|---------------------|-----|----|-------|-------|
| Comfort             | 40  | 96 | 12    | 148   |
| Cost                | 108 | 68 | 8     | 184   |
| Performance         | 62  | 62 | 12    | 136   |
| Reliability         | 128 | 116| 4     | 248   |
| Safety              | 152 | 192| 32    | 376   |
| Total               | 490 | 534| 68    | 1,092 |

Which of the following is closest to the proportion of people who responded no to color consideration and who identified safety as the additional feature that was important?

Scientists estimate that the distribution of the life span of the Galápagos Islands giant tortoise is approximately normal with mean 100 years and standard deviation 15 years. Based on the estimate, which of the following is closest to the age of a Galápagos Islands giant tortoise at the 90th percentile of the distribution?

options.A: 80 years
options.B: 115 years
options.C: 120 years
options.D: 125 years
options.E: 130 years

text: For the purpose of determining the value of its end-of-year inventory, a clothing store creates a list at the end of the year of every item currently in stock along with each item’s wholesale price. Which of the following is the best description of the end-of-year activity?

options.A: An experiment, because the items are treatments and wholesale prices are responses.
options.B: An experiment, because the store does not know the total wholesale price of all the items.
options.C: A sample survey, because the store wants to estimate the value of all items for the entire year.
options.D: A sample survey, because the items currently in the store at the end of the year are a random sample of all items in the store for the entire year.
options.E: A census, because the wholesale prices of all items are listed.

text:
A grocery store receives deliveries of corn from two farms, one in Iowa and the other in Ohio. Both farms
produce ears of corn with mean weight 1.26 pounds. The standard deviation of the weights of the ears of corn
from the farm in Ohio is 0.01 pound greater than that from the farm in Iowa. A randomly selected ear of corn
from the farm in Iowa weighed 1.39 pounds, which has a standardized score of 1.645 for the distribution of
weights for the Iowa corn. If an ear of corn from the farm in Ohio weighs 1.39 pounds, how many standard
deviations from the mean is the weight with respect to the Ohio distribution?

options.A: 1.46 standard deviations below the mean
options.B: 1.46 standard deviations above the mean
options.C: 1.65 standard deviations above the mean
options.D: 1.88 standard deviations below the mean
options.E: 1.88 standard deviations above the mean

text:
The distribution of number of hours worked by volunteers last year at a large hospital is approximately normal
with mean 80 and standard deviation 7. Volunteers in the top 20 percent of hours worked will receive a
certificate of merit. If a volunteer from last year is selected at random, which of the following is closest to the
probability that the volunteer selected will receive a certificate of merit given that the number of hours the
volunteer worked is less than 90 ?

Resting heart rates, in beats per minute, were recorded for two samples of people. One sample was from people in the age-group of 20 years to 30 years, and the other sample was from people in the age-group of 40 years to 50 years. The five-number summaries are shown in the table.

| Age-Group (years) | Minimum | Q1 | Median | Q3 | Maximum |
|-------------------|---------|----|--------|----|---------|
| 20 to 30          | 60      | 71 | 72     | 75 | 84      |
| 40 to 50          | 60      | 70 | 73     | 76 | 85      |

The values of 60, 62, and 84 were common to both samples. The three values are identified as outliers with respect to the age-group 20 years to 30 years because they are either 1.5 times the interquartile range (IQR) greater than the upper quartile or 1.5 times the IQR less than the lower quartile.

Using the same method for identifying outliers, which of the three values are identified as outliers for the age-group 40 years to 50 years?

options.A: None of the three values is identified as an outlier.
options.B: Only 60 is identified as an outlier.
options.C: Only 60 and 62 are identified as outliers.
options.D: Only 60 and 84 are identified as outliers.
options.E: The three values are all identified as outliers.

text: As part of a demographic study, a college administrator needed to survey a sample of students from the college. From each major offered at the college, the administrator randomly selected 5 percent of the students with that major to participate in the survey. Which of the following is the best description of the type of sample selected by the administrator?

options.A: Cluster sample
options.B: Convenience sample
options.C: Simple random sample
options.D: Stratified random sample
options.E: Systematic random sample

text: A biologist studying trees constructed the confidence interval (0.14, 0.20) to estimate the proportion of trees in a large forest that are dead but still standing. Using the same confidence level, the interval was later revised because the sample proportion had been miscalculated. The correct sample proportion was 0.27. Which of the following statements about the revised interval based on the correct sample proportion is true?

options.A: The revised interval is narrower than the original interval because the correct sample proportion is farther from 0.5 than the miscalculated proportion is.
options.B: The revised interval is narrower than the original interval because the correct sample proportion is closer to 0.5 than the miscalculated proportion is.
options.C: The revised interval is wider than the original interval because the correct sample proportion is farther from 0.5 than the miscalculated proportion is.
options.D: The revised interval is wider than the original interval because the correct sample proportion is closer to 0.5 than the miscalculated proportion is.
options.E: The revised interval has the same width as the original interval.

text: A research organization reported that 41 percent of adults who were asked to describe their day responded that they were having a good day rather than a typical day or a bad day. To investigate whether the percent would be different for high school students, 600 high school students were randomly selected. When asked to describe their day, 245 students reported that they were having a good day rather than a typical day or a bad day. Do the data provide convincing statistical evidence that the proportion of all high school students who would respond that they were having a good day is different from 0.41 ?

options.A: No, because the p-value is less than any reasonable significance level.
options.B: No, because the p-value is greater than any reasonable significance level.
options.C: Yes, because the p-value is less than any reasonable significance level.
options.D: Yes, because the p-value is greater than any reasonable significance level.
options.E: Yes, because the expected value of the number of students who will report having a good day is 246, not 245.

text: The director of a community recreation center conducted a six-week study to examine the effects of four types of exercise—strength training, flexibility training, aerobics, and jogging—on maximal oxygen consumption. From the 40 members who participated, the director randomly assigned 10 members to each exercise type. Maximal oxygen consumption was measured for each member at the beginning of the study and again at the end of the six weeks. The director examined the change in maximal oxygen consumption for each member. Which of the following statements is a correct description of a feature of the study?

options.A: The study has replication because there are four types of exercise.
options.B: The study has replication because it was conducted over a six-week period.
options.C: The response variable is the type of exercise with the greatest change in maximal oxygen consumption.
options.D: The treatments in the study are strength training, flexibility training, aerobics, and jogging.
options.E: The experimental units are the four different types of exercise.

text: Sean and Evan are college roommates who have part-time jobs as servers in restaurants. The distribution of Sean’s weekly income is approximately normal with mean $225 and standard deviation $25. The distribution of Evan’s weekly income is approximately normal with mean $240 and standard deviation $15. Assuming their weekly incomes are independent of each other, which of the following is closest to the probability that Sean will have a greater income than Evan in a randomly selected week?

According to data from the United States Elections Project, only 36 percent of eligible voters voted in the 2014 elections. For random samples of size 40, which of the following best describes the sampling distribution of p̂, the sample proportion of people who voted in the 2014 elections?

options.A: The sampling distribution is skewed to the left, with mean 0.36 and standard deviation 0.076.
options.B: The sampling distribution is skewed to the right, with mean 0.64 and standard deviation 0.006.
options.C: The sampling distribution is approximately normal, with mean 0.36 and standard deviation 0.076.
options.D: The sampling distribution is approximately normal, with mean 0.36 and standard deviation 0.006.
options.E: The sampling distribution is approximately normal, with mean 0.64 and standard deviation 0.076.

text: To investigate whether the consumption of beetroot juice enhances exercise performance, a researcher selected a random sample of 50 student athletes from all the student athletes at a college. The athletes in the sample were randomly assigned to one of two groups. In one group, 25 athletes were given a daily dose of beetroot juice, and in the other group, the remaining athletes were given a daily dose of a placebo. At the end of six weeks of exercise training, the researcher compared the performances of the two groups. Based on the design of the investigation, which of the following is the largest population to which the results can be generalized?

options.A: The 25 student athletes assigned to the beetroot juice group
options.B: The 50 student athletes in the sample
options.C: All student athletes at the college
options.D: All students at the college
options.E: All people who exercise

text:
College researchers wanted to know under what conditions people are more likely to complete and return a survey. As part of a study, the researchers prepared three sets of identical surveys and used three methods of delivering and returning the surveys. The methods are described as follows.
• In Class: The surveys were given to students in a class, and students were asked to return completed surveys to their instructor.
• Psychology: The surveys were given to students participating in a psychology experiment, and students were asked to return completed surveys to a collection box in the hallway of the psychology building.
• Dining Hall: The surveys were given to students in the dining hall, and students were asked to return completed surveys to a collection box outside the dining hall.
The graph shows the percent of surveys returned and not returned for each delivery method.
[Bar graph with three horizontal bars labeled "In Class", "Psychology", and "Dining Hall". Each bar is divided into two segments: a light gray segment labeled "Returned the survey" and a dark gray segment labeled "Did not return the survey". The x-axis is labeled "Rate of Return" and ranges from 0% to 100%. The light gray segment for "In Class" extends to about 60%. The light gray segment for "Psychology" extends to about 40%. The light gray segment for "Dining Hall" extends to about 30%.]
Which statement about delivery method and rate of survey return is supported by the graph?

options.A: There is a positive association between delivery method and rate of return.
options.B: There is a negative association between delivery method and rate of return.
options.C: The number of surveys given using the Dining Hall delivery method was less than the number given using either of the other delivery methods.
options.D: The Psychology delivery method displays the most symmetric results; the other delivery methods display skewed results.
options.E:
The In Class delivery method had the greatest rate of return, and the Dining Hall delivery method had the least rate of return.

text: The height and age of each child in a random sample of children was recorded. The value of the correlation coefficient between height and age for the children in the sample was 0.8. Based on the least-squares regression line created from the data to predict the height of a child based on age, which of the following is a correct statement?

options.A: On average, the height of a child is 80% of the age of the child.
options.B: The least-squares regression line of height versus age will have a slope of 0.8.
options.C: The proportion of the variation in height that is explained by a regression on age is 0.64.
options.D: The least-squares regression line will correctly predict height based on age 80% of the time.
options.E: The least-squares regression line will correctly predict height based on age 64% of the time.

text: At a certain restaurant, the distribution of wait times between ordering a meal and receiving the meal has mean 11.4 minutes and standard deviation 2.6 minutes. The restaurant manager wants to find the probability that the mean wait time will be greater than 12.0 minutes for a random sample of 84 customers. Assuming the wait times among customers are independent, which of the following describes the sampling distribution of the sample mean wait time for random samples of size 84 ?

options.A: Approximately normal with mean 11.4 minutes and standard deviation 2.6 minutes
options.B: Approximately normal with mean 11.4 minutes and standard deviation $\frac{2.6}{\sqrt{84}}$ minute
options.C: Approximately normal with mean 12.0 minutes and standard deviation 2.6 minutes
options.D: Binomial with mean $84(0.41)$ minutes and standard deviation $\sqrt{84(0.41)(0.59)}$ minutes
options.E: Binomial with mean $84(0.5)$ minutes and standard deviation $\sqrt{84(0.5)(0.5)}$ minutes

text: Two high schools have a similar number of students and parking lots of similar size. The safety officers at both schools want to investigate whether there is an average difference in the number of cars parked per day in the student parking lots for the school year. A random sample of 15 school days will be selected. For each selected day, the number of cars parked in the student parking lots will be counted at both schools and the difference will be recorded. Assuming all conditions for inference are met, which of the following is the appropriate test for the investigation?

options.A: A two-sample z-test for a difference between proportions
options.B: A two-sample t-test for a difference between means
options.C: A matched-pairs t-test for a mean difference
options.D: A chi-square test of homogeneity
options.E: A chi-square test of independence

A state study on labor reported that one-third of full-time teachers in the state also worked part time at another job. For those teachers, the average number of hours worked per week at the part-time job was 13. After an increase in state teacher salaries, a random sample of 400 teachers who worked part time at another job was selected. The average number of hours worked per week at the part-time job for the teachers in the sample was 12.5 with standard deviation 6.5 hours. Is there convincing statistical evidence, at the level of α = 0.05, that the average number of hours worked per week at part-time jobs decreased after the salary increase?

options.A: No. The p-value of the appropriate test is greater than 0.05.
options.B: No. The p-value of the appropriate test is less than 0.05.
options.C: Yes. The p-value of the appropriate test is greater than 0.05.
options.D: Yes. The p-value of the appropriate test is less than 0.05.
options.E: Not enough information is given to determine whether there is convincing statistical evidence.

text:
A city department of transportation studied traffic congestion on a certain highway. To encourage carpooling, the department will recommend a carpool lane if the average number of people in passenger cars on the highway is less than 2. The probability distribution of the number of people in passenger cars on the highway is shown in the table.

| Number of people | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|------------------|---|---|---|---|---|
| Probability      | 0.56 | 0.28 | 0.08 | 0.06 | 0.02 |

Based on the probability distribution, what is the mean number of people in passengers cars on the highway?

To compare the effectiveness of two treatments, researchers conducted a well-designed experiment using a randomized block design in which the subjects were blocked by age-group (under 40 years and 40 years or older). Which of the following must be true about the randomized block design of the experiment?

options.A: The number of subjects in each block is different.
options.B: Treatments are randomly assigned to subjects within each block.
options.C: The design cannot have a control group because subjects are blocked by age-group.
options.D: The experiment uses a matched-pairs design, where subjects from one block are paired with subjects from the other block.
options.E: The subjects in one block receive one treatment, and the subjects in the other block receive the other treatment.

text:
A die used in a certain board game has eight faces, of which 3 are red, 3 are yellow, and 2 are blue. Each face is equally likely to land faceup when the die is tossed. In the game, a player tosses the die until blue lands faceup, and the number of tosses before blue lands faceup is counted. For example, a player who tosses the sequence shown in the following table has tossed the die 3 times before blue lands faceup.

| Toss number | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Face color | yellow | yellow | red | blue |

What is the probability that a player will toss the die at least 2 times before blue lands faceup?

A statistical test involves the following null and alternative hypotheses.

H₀: μ = 64
Hₐ: μ > 64

Which of the following describes a Type II error?

options.A: Failing to reject the null hypothesis when the population mean is 64
options.B: Failing to reject the null hypothesis when the population mean is greater than 64
options.C: Rejecting the null hypothesis when the population mean is 64
options.D: Rejecting the null hypothesis when the population mean is greater than 64
options.E: Failing to reject the null hypothesis when the p-value is less than the significance level

text: The marketing director for an ice cream company investigated whether there was a difference in preference for two new ice cream flavors—cotton candy and mango. Each participant from a large group of people was randomly assigned to taste one of the two flavors. After tasting, each person rated the flavor on a numerical scale from 1 to 5, where 1 represented strongly dislike and 5 represented strongly like. A two-sample t-interval for a difference between means (cotton candy minus mango) was constructed. Based on the interval, there was convincing statistical evidence of a difference in population mean flavor ratings, with mango having the greater sample mean rating. Which of the following could be the constructed interval?

The director of a marketing department wants to estimate the proportion of people who purchase a certain product online. The director originally planned to obtain a random sample of 2,500 people who purchased the product. However, because of budget concerns, the sample size will be reduced to 1,500 people. Which of the following describes the effect of reducing the number of people in the sample?

options.A: The variance of the sample will increase.
options.B: The variance of the population will decrease.
options.C: The variance of the sampling distribution of the estimator will increase.
options.D: The variance of the sampling distribution of the estimator will decrease.
options.E: The variance of the sampling distribution of the estimator will remain the same.

text: From a random sample of 1,005 adults in the United States, it was found that 32 percent own an e-reader. Which of the following is the appropriate 90 percent confidence interval to estimate the proportion of all adults in the United States who own an e-reader?

Measuring the height of a tree is usually more difficult than measuring the diameter of the tree. Therefore, many researchers use regression models to predict the height of a tree from its diameter measured at 4 feet 6 inches from the ground. The following computer output shows the results of a linear regression based on the heights, in feet, and the diameters, in inches, recorded from 31 felled trees.

|             | Estimate | Std Error | t value | Pr(>|t|) |
|-------------|----------|-----------|---------|----------|
| Intercept   | 62.031   | 4.383     | 14.15   | 0.0000   |
| Diameter    | 1.054    | 0.322     | 3.27    | 0.0028   |

Which of the following is a 95 percent confidence interval for the slope of the population regression line?

A group of 80 people who had been diagnosed as prediabetic because of high blood glucose levels volunteered to participate in a study designed to investigate the use of cinnamon to reduce blood glucose to a normal level. Of the 80 people, 40 were randomly assigned to take a cinnamon tablet each day and the other 40 were assigned to take a placebo each day. The people did not know which tablet they were taking. Their blood glucose levels were measured at the end of one month. The results showed that 14 people in the cinnamon group and 10 people in the placebo group had normal blood glucose levels. For people similar to those in the study, do the data provide convincing statistical evidence that the proportion who would be classified as normal after one month of taking cinnamon is greater than the proportion who would be classified as normal after one month of not taking cinnamon?

options.A: No conclusion can be made about the use of cinnamon because the people in the study were volunteers.
options.B: There is convincing statistical evidence at the level of 0.01.
options.C: There is convincing statistical evidence at the level of 0.05 but not at the level of 0.01.
options.D: There is convincing statistical evidence at the level of 0.10 but not at the level of 0.05.
options.E: There is not convincing statistical evidence at any reasonable significance level.

text:
A student working on a physics project investigated the relationship between the speed and the height of roller coasters. The student collected data on the maximum speed, in miles per hour, and the maximum height, in feet, for a random sample of 21 roller coasters, with the intent of testing the slope of the linear relationship between maximum speed and maximum height. However, based on the residual plot shown, the conditions for such a test might not be met.

Based on the residual plot, which condition appears to have been violated?

options.A: The errors are independent.
options.B: The sum of the residuals is 0.
options.C: The expected value of the errors is 0.
options.D: There is a linear relationship between the response variable and the explanatory variable.
options.E: The variance of the response variable is constant for all values of the explanatory variable.

text: A researcher conducted a t-test of the hypotheses H₀ : μ = 38 versus Hₐ : μ ≠ 38. The sample mean was 35 and the p-value for the test was 0.0627. What would the p-value have been if the researcher had used Hₐ : μ < 38 as the alternative hypothesis?

High school students from track teams in the state participated in a training program to improve running times.
Before the training, the mean running time for the students to run a mile was 402 seconds with standard
deviation 40 seconds. After completing the program, the mean running time for the students to run a mile was
368 seconds with standard deviation 30 seconds. Let X represent the running time of a randomly selected
student before training, and let Y represent the running time of the same student after training. Which of the
following is true about the distribution of X − Y ?

options.A:
The variables X and Y are independent; therefore, the mean is 34 seconds and the standard deviation is
10 seconds.
options.B:
The variables X and Y are independent; therefore, the mean is 34 seconds and the standard deviation is
50 seconds.
options.C:
The variables X and Y are not independent; therefore, the standard deviation is 50 seconds and the mean
cannot be determined with the information given.
options.D:
The variables X and Y are not independent; therefore, the mean is 34 seconds and the standard deviation
cannot be determined with the information given.
options.E:
The variables X and Y are not independent; therefore, neither the mean nor the standard deviation can be
determined with the information given.

text: A polling organization surveyed 2,002 randomly selected adults who are not scientists and 3,748 randomly selected adults who are scientists. Each adult was asked the question "Do you think that genetically modified foods are safe to eat?" Of those who are not scientists, 37 percent responded yes, and of those who are scientists, 88 percent responded yes. Which of the following is the standard error used to construct a confidence interval for the difference between the proportions of all adults who are not scientists and all adults who are scientists who would answer yes to the question?

A polling agency conducted a survey about social media in which each person in random samples of 1,000 men and 1,000 women was asked what factor he or she considers to be the most important when deciding whether to connect on social media with another person. The responses are shown in the table.

| Factor               | Personal Friend | Stay in Touch | Mutual Friends | Business Networking | Other |
|----------------------|-----------------|---------------|----------------|---------------------|-------|
| Men                  | 600             | 210           | 105            | 45                  | 40    |
| Women                | 650             | 224           | 65             | 15                  | 46    |

What is the contribution to the chi-square test statistic for men who selected business networking as the most important factor?

A national survey asked 1,501 randomly selected employed adults how many hours they work per week. Based on the collected data, a 95 percent confidence interval for the mean number of hours worked per week for all employed adults was given as (41.18, 42.63). Which of the following statements is a correct interpretation of the interval?

options.A: Ninety-five percent of all employed adults work between 41.18 hours and 42.63 hours per week.
options.B: The probability is 0.95 that a sample of size 1,501 will produce a mean between 41.18 hours and 42.63 hours.
options.C: Of all samples of size 1,501 taken from the population, 95% of the samples will have a mean between 41.18 hours and 42.63 hours.
options.D: We are 95% confident that the mean number of hours worked per week for employed adults in the sample is between 41.18 hours and 42.63 hours.
options.E: We are 95% confident that the mean number of hours worked per week for all employed adults is between 41.18 hours and 42.63 hours.

Arsenic is a naturally occurring chemical that can enter groundwater through eroding granite or from a burned forest. A health organization recommends drinking water should contain no more than 10 parts per billion (ppb) of arsenic. A company produces filters to clean arsenic from private wells that could be affected by the groundwater.

The company wants to investigate the effectiveness of a new filter compared to that of an older filter. They will test the filters on a field that is bordered on one side by a granite ledge and on the other side by a burned forest. The field is divided into 8 square plots of equal size, and a well to collect groundwater is drilled in the center of each plot. One filter will be used in each well. The following diagram shows the placement of the wells in the field.

[Diagram: 2 rows of 4 wells each, labeled Well 1 to Well 8. Top row above the wells is labeled "Granite Ledge" and bottom row below the wells is labeled "Burned Forest".]

The company will use four of each type of filter to conduct the investigation. A randomized block design will be used.

(a) Assuming there is a difference in the effectiveness of the two filters, under what conditions will a randomized block design be better for detecting the difference than a completely randomized design?

(b) Identify the wells, by number, that will be included in each block.

(c) Describe how to assign filters to wells to create a randomized block design.

Question 2

Intent of Question
The primary goal of this question were to assess a student’s ability to (1) construct and interpret a confidence interval for the slope of a regression line; and (2) determine if the confidence interval contradicts or supports a prior belief/claim.

Solution:
(a) The 95 percent confidence interval for the slope is computed as follows:
-2.158 ± t18,0.975 × (0.149) = -2.158 ± (2.101) × (0.149)
= (-2.471, -1.845)

We are 95% confident that the slope of the true regression line is between -2.471 and -1.845 thousands of dollars per mile. This implies that for each additional mile that a three-bedroom house is away from the city center, the selling price of the house is expected to decline between $1,845 and $2,471.

(b) Because the confidence interval contains -2, corresponding to a $2,000 decrease, it is a plausible value for the slope of the regression line. Consequently, the data do not contradict the agent’s belief that the selling prices of three bedroom houses decrease about $2,000 for every one-mile increase in the distance of the house from the city center.

Scoring
This question is scored in three sections. Section 1 consists of computing the 95% confidence interval for the slope of the true regression line in part (a). Section 2 consists of the interpretation of the confidence interval in part (a). Section 3 consists of the response to part (b). Sections 1, 2, and 3 are each scored as essentially correct (E), partially correct (P), or incorrect (I).

Section 1 is scored as follows:

Essentially correct (E) if the confidence interval displays the form
(estimated slope) ± critical value × SE(estimated slope)
AND
correct values are used for the estimated slope (-2.158), the standard error for the estimated slope (0.149), and the 0.975 percentile of the t-distribution with 18 degrees of freedom (2.101)
AND
correct values are reported for the endpoints of the confidence interval.

Partially correct (P) if the response displays the form
(estimated slope) ± critical value × SE(estimated slope), but uses an incorrect value for one of the following: the t-percentile, the estimate of the slope, or the standard error of the slope.
OR
if the response gives the correct endpoints of the confidence interval but does not provide sufficient work.

Incorrect if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Question 3

Intent of Question
The primary goals of this question were to assess a student’s ability to (1) extract information about counts from a histogram; (2) use histograms to compare distributions; (3) estimate a median from the combined information in two histograms.

Solution

Part (a):
(i) Three sites were vacated before 8:30 A.M. This is the sum of the counts represented by the two leftmost bars of the histogram of exit times for campsites without young children. No campers with small children vacated their campsites before 8:30 A.M.
(ii) Eight sites were vacated at 11:00 A.M. or later. This is the sum of the counts represented by the two rightmost bars on each histogram.

Part (b):
The distribution of exit times for campers without young children is skewed to the left while the distribution for campers with young children is roughly symmetric. Both exit time distributions appear to be unimodal. The distribution of exit times is more spread out for campers without young children; the largest possible range is around 210 minutes compared to a largest possible range of around 105 minutes for campers with young children. Campers without young children tend to leave the campground earlier than campers with young children; the median exit time for campers without young children is between 60 and 75 minutes after 9:00 A.M., which is less than the median exit time for campers with young children, which is between 90 and 105 minutes after 9:00 A.M.

Part (c)
There are a total of 60 exit times, so the median falls between the 30th and 31st exit times. Since the 30th and 31st exit times fall between 10:15 and 10:30, any time between 10:15 A.M. and 10:30 A.M. provides a reasonable estimate of the median exit time.

Scoring
Parts (a), (b) and (c) are each scored as essentially correct (E), partially correct (P), or incorrect (I).

Part (a) is scored as follows:
Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following two components:
Correctly states that 3 sites were vacated before 8:30 A.M. in part (i).

Correctly states that 8 sites were vacated at 11:00 A.M. or later in part (ii)

Partially correct (P) if the response contains only one of the two components.
OR
Correctly estimates the counts at each site separately, but does not combine them for a total count.

Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Question 4

Intent of Question
The primary goals of this question were to assess a student’s ability to (1) identify conditions under which a randomized block design would be better than a completely randomized design for detecting a difference in mean responses for two treatments, and (2) describe how an experiment can be conducted as a randomized block design.

Solution

Part (a):
A randomized block experiment will be better for detecting a difference between the abilities of the new and old filters to reduce arsenic concentrations when (1) there is at least one feature of the wells that enable the researchers to create blocks such that each block consists of wells with similar arsenic concentrations and (2) the average arsenic concentration varies among blocks. This would occur, for example, when well water arsenic concentrations at all four wells near the burned forest are higher than the arsenic concentration at any well near the granite ledge.

Part (b):
To take advantage of the differences among wells, well water arsenic concentrations must be similar for wells with each block, but average arsenic concentrations should vary among blocks. A randomized block experiment with two blocks could be conducted by including wells 1, 2, 3, and 4, on the granite ledge side of the field in one block and including wells 5, 6, 7, and 8, on the burned forest side of the field in the other block.

Part (c)
Within each block, the new filter should be randomly assigned to the same number of wells as the old filter. For the blocks identified in part (b), this can be accomplished by writing well numbers 1, 2, 3, and 4 on four slips of paper, placing the four slips into a hat, and mixing them. New filters would be used for the wells on the first two slips drawn from the hat and old filters would be used for the other two wells. Similarly, well numbers 5, 6, 7, and 8 can be written on four slips of paper that are put into a hat and mixed. New filters would be used for the wells on the first two slips drawn from the hat and old filters would be used for the other two wells.

Visit the College Board on the web: collegeboard.org.
