To estimate the proportion of Europeans who walk for exercise at least 10 minutes a week, data were collected from a random sample of 27,919 people from Europe. The 95 percent confidence interval created from the data was (0.856, 0.864).

Which of the following is an appropriate interpretation of the confidence interval?

options.A: The probability that a randomly selected person from Europe walks for exercise at least 10 minutes a week is 0.95.
options.B: The proportion of Europeans who walk between 0.856 and 0.864 minute a week for exercise is 0.95.
options.C: There is a 95% chance that the sample proportion of Europeans who walk for exercise at least 10 minutes a week is between 0.856 and 0.864.
options.D: Between 85.6% and 86.4% of all Europeans walk for exercise at least 10 minutes a week.
options.E: We can be 95% confident that the proportion of all Europeans who walk for exercise at least 10 minutes a week is between 0.856 and 0.864.

text:
A study was conducted to investigate whether there is a difference in the mean life span of a new brand of battery compared with an older brand of battery. Independent random samples of 9 new-brand batteries and 8 older-brand batteries were selected, and the life span (in hours) of each battery was recorded. Summary statistics obtained from the data are shown in the table below. Assume that the conditions for inference are met.

|          | New Brand | Older Brand |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Mean     | 426.8     | 355.2       |
| Standard Deviation | 49.5 | 96.7       |

Which of the following best approximates the p-value for a test of H₀: μ_new - μ_older = 0 versus H_a: μ_new - μ_older ≠ 0, where μ_new is the population mean life span of the new brand of battery and μ_older is the population mean life span of the older brand of battery?

The campaign manager for a candidate for governor wants to estimate the difference between the percentage of voters who viewed an advertisement and favor the candidate and the percentage of voters who had not viewed the advertisement and favor the candidate. Two independent random samples of 500 voters were selected. One random sample consisted of voters who viewed the advertisement and the other random sample consisted of voters who had not viewed the advertisement. The table shows the frequencies of responses for those surveyed.

|                | Have Not Seen Advertisement | Have Seen Advertisement |
|----------------|-----------------------------|-------------------------|
| In favor       | 224                         | 283                     |
| Not in favor   | 276                         | 217                     |

Which of the following is the most appropriate method for the campaign manager to use to estimate the difference between the percentages of voters who favor the candidate?

options.A: A one-sample z-interval for estimating a population proportion
options.B: A t-interval for estimating the slope of the least-squares regression line
options.C: A matched-pairs t-interval for estimating a mean difference
options.D: A two-sample z-interval for estimating a difference between sample proportions
options.E: A two-sample z-interval for estimating a difference between population proportions

text:
A national newspaper reported that 40 percent of high school students engage in community service activities. A school administrator wondered whether the proportion of high school students at the administrator’s school who engage in community service activities is less than 40 percent. The administrator selected a random sample of 100 students, and 36 responded that they do engage in community service activities. The conditions for inference were met, and an appropriate hypothesis test resulted in a p-value of 0.207.

Which of the following is a correct interpretation of this p-value?

options.A: The probability that a randomly selected student at this high school engages in community service activities is 0.207.
options.B: The probability is 0.40 that less than 20.7 percent of students at this high school engage in community service activities.
options.C: The probability is 0.207 that less than 40 percent of students at this high school engage in community service activities.
options.D: If the proportion of all students at the school who engage in community service activities is 0.4, the probability of observing 36 or fewer in a random sample of 100 students who engage in community service activities is 0.207.
options.E: If the proportion of all students at the school who engage in community service activities is 0.36, the probability of obtaining 40 or fewer in a random sample of 100 who engage in community service activities is 0.207.

text:
The editor of a local newspaper wants to know whether readers would be willing to pay an additional $1.00 per month for their monthly newspaper subscription in exchange for an extra weekly section featuring stories about local businesses. The editor plans to survey a random sample of readers, and if the editor finds convincing statistical evidence that more than 75 percent of readers support the change, the editor will implement the plan of starting the extra weekly section.

In the context of this situation, which of the following describes a consequence of a Type I error?

options.A: The editor decides to implement the plan of starting the extra weekly section when the actual percentage of readers who support the change is greater than 75%.
options.B: The editor decides to implement the plan of starting the extra weekly section when the actual percentage of readers who support the change is not greater than 75%.
options.C: The editor decides to implement the plan of starting the extra weekly section when the actual percentage of readers who support the change is less than 50%.
options.D: The editor decides not to implement the plan of starting the extra weekly section when the actual percentage of readers who support the change is greater than 75%.
options.E: The editor decides not to implement the plan of starting the extra weekly section when the actual percentage of readers who support the change is not greater than 75%.

text: Which statement about the power of a significance test is true?

options.A: A test performed at a significance level of α = 0.01 has a higher power than the same test performed at a α = 0.05.
options.B: If the probability of Type I error is decreased, the power of the test is increased.
options.C: The power of a test is equal to the probability of making a Type II error.
options.D: The power of a test is independent of the sample size.
options.E: The power of a test at a particular parameter value increases as the distance from the hypothesized value to the parameter value increases.

text:
A researcher in sports medicine is interested in comparing the amount of daily screen time between high school students who are athletes and high school students who are not athletes. A random sample of 100 high school students who are athletes and a random sample of 100 high school students who are not athletes will be selected. Each student will be asked “Do you typically have more than 3 hours of screen time each day?”

Which of the following is the most appropriate test for the researcher to use to investigate whether there is a difference in the amounts of daily screen time?

options.A: A two-sample z-test for a difference between population proportions
options.B: A two-sample t-test for a difference between population proportions
options.C: A two-sample z-test for a difference between population means
options.D: A two-sample t-test for a difference between population means
options.E: A chi-square goodness-of-fit test

text:
Researchers studying the relationship between sleep and memory obtained a sample of 28 adults to participate in an experiment. Half of the participants were randomly assigned to a full night’s sleep, and the other half were awakened every two hours throughout the night. The following day, the participants were given a list of words to memorize and recall after one hour. Researchers recorded the number of words recalled by each participant. The p-value of an appropriate statistical test was 0.29.

Which of the following is a reasonable conclusion?

options.A: The data provide sufficient evidence that memory is related to sleep.
options.B: The data provide sufficient evidence that memory is not related to sleep.
options.C: A cause-and-effect relationship between memory and sleep cannot be established. However, the data provide sufficient evidence that there is an association between memory and sleep.
options.D: The data do not provide convincing evidence that memory is related to sleep.
options.E: The conditions for inference must not have been satisfied.

text:
A recent report stated that 30 percent of smartphone users exceed their data limit in a given month. A consumer advocacy group questions the validity of the reported percent. The group obtained a random sample of 100 smartphone users and found that 40 of them exceeded their data limit in a certain month.

Of the following sampling distributions, which one should be used to test the hypothesis that the proportion is different than 30 percent?

Solution

Part (a):

The least-squares estimate of the slope of Model I is 1.032 clips/cm. The predicted (or average) number of paper clips that can be grabbed by the claw increases by about 1.032 clips for each one-centimeter increase in the span of the claw.

Scoring

Identifies the value of the slope as 1.032

Solution

Part (b):

The least-squares estimate of the intercept of Model I is 3.179 clips. This corresponds to the predicted (or average) number of paper clips that can be grabbed by a claw with a span of zero centimeters. A predicted value of 3.179 clips is not sensible because a claw with a span of zero centimeters could not exist and would not be able to pick up any paper clips.

Identifies the value of the intercept as 3.179 OR indicates that the intercept is positive

Explains that a claw with a span of zero centimeters could not exist

Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies only two of the three components.

Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Component 1 can be satisfied by stating that the claw could not grab any paper clips.

The Model II residual is

(ii) Model II provides a better prediction (a prediction closer to the 20 paper clips that were actually grabbed with a claw of size 18 cm in this study) because the residual for Model II is closer to zero than the residual for Model I.

if the response correctly calculates both residuals but does not provide supporting work or justification AND satisfies at least one of component 3 or component 4.

Researchers at a large university with a population of 20,000 students are investigating student opinions about the university fitness center. A list of the names of students and how often they use the fitness center is available.

Two methods of stratification are being considered for selecting a sample of 120 students.

Method I - Stratify by how often students use the fitness center (no usage, infrequent usage, and frequent usage).

Method II - Stratify by first letter of students’ last names (A to F, G to M, N to S, and T to Z).

(a) Which of the two methods for creating the strata, method I or method II, should the researchers use for their investigation? Explain your answer.

(b) Describe a procedure that could be used to select a stratified random sample of 120 students at the university for the method selected in part (a). The procedure should result in a sample that consists of an equal number of students from each stratum.

Solution
Part (a):
The researchers should use method I for creating the strata in their investigation since it would be better to stratify by how often students use the fitness center than by first letter of last name. Stratified random sampling is best used with homogeneous strata, that is, when variation in responses is relatively small within strata. There is no reason to believe that forming groups of students based on the first letter of their last name would result in very homogeneous strata with respect to opinions about the fitness center. On the other hand, students who frequently use the fitness center may have similar opinions, and those opinions may differ substantially from opinions of students who never use it, or students who use it infrequently. Consequently, method I would produce strata with smaller within-strata variation in opinions about the fitness center.
Scoring
Part (a) is scored as follows:
Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following three components:
Indicates that method I should be used
Addresses EITHER homogeneity within groups (e.g., "students in the same group have similar opinions") OR heterogeneity between groups (e.g., "students in different groups tend to have different opinions")
Relates the stratification variable to the response variable in context (e.g., "how often students use the gym may affect their opinion")
Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies component 1 AND component 2
OR
If the response satisfies component 1 AND 3,
OR
if the response does not select either method AND satisfies both component 2 and component 3.
Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.
Additional Notes:
· Examples of responses that earn a P
o "Method 1 because it creates homogenous groups" satisfies components 1 and 2.
o "Method 1 because the first letter of students' last names likely has nothing to do with opinions about the fitness center" satisfies components 1 and 3.

o "It is important to select the method that creates the most similar groups of students in terms of their opinions about the fitness center" (satisfies components 2 and 3)

· Note that a response that selects Method 2 does not meet the criteria for E or P and scores I.

Solution

Part (b):

Break up the list of all 20,000 students at the university into three separate lists corresponding to the three strata for method I – one for students who never use the fitness center, one for students who infrequently use the fitness center, and one for students who frequently use the fitness center. Select separate random samples of size 40 from each of the three lists. Start with the list of students who never use the fitness center. If there are N₁ students on the list, number the students from 1 to N₁ based on their position on the list. Using a random number generator, generate 40 random numbers between 1 and N₁, without repeats. These numbers indicate which students should be included in the sample from the first stratum. Repeat this procedure to draw a random sample of 40 students from the N₂ students on the second list and a random sample of 40 students from the N₃ students on the third list.

Scoring

Part (b) is scored as follows:

Essentially correct (E) if the response is consistent with the method selected in (a) or with the correct method AND satisfies the following four components:

Selects a separate sample from each stratum (3 strata for method I, or 4 strata for method II)

Identifies appropriate labels for students, such as numbering the students from 1 to N where N is the number of students in a stratum, for each sample (stratum)

Describes how to implement a process for randomly selecting students from a stratum, such that every student in the stratum has an equal chance of being selected

The random selection process results in the same number of students being selected from each stratum (40 per stratum for method I, or 30 per stratum for method II)

Partially correct (P) if the response is consistent with the method selected in (a) or with the correct method AND satisfies only two or three of the four components

OR

if the response is not consistent with the method in (a) AND stratifies on a variable other than frequency of gym usage AND satisfies components two and three. (E.g., stratifying on class standing – freshperson, sophomore, junior senior.)

OR

if the response describes a sampling method other than stratified (e.g., systematic or SRS) that identifies appropriate labels for sampling units in context (e.g., students or groups of students) AND describes how to implement a process for randomly selecting units such that every unit has an equal chance of being selected.

Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Additional Notes:

A farm produces olive oil that is put into bottles that are sealed with a cork. The diameter of the bottle opening is 1.03 inches. To provide an acceptable fit, the cork diameter should be from 0.99 inch to 1.03 inches. The corks are purchased in bulk from a supplier, and the distribution of cork diameters is approximately normal with mean 1.00 inch and standard deviation 0.05 inch.

(a) If one cork is selected at random, what is the probability that the cork selected will provide an acceptable fit for the bottle

A farm manager decides to purchase the corks from a new supplier. Seventy percent of corks produced by the new supplier have a diameter with an acceptable fit.

(i) Define the random variable of interest and state how the random variable is distributed.

Solution
Part (a):
Let the random variable D represent the diameter of a randomly selected cork. The distribution of D is approximately normal with mean 1.00 inch and standard deviation 0.05 inch. The probability that a randomly selected cork will provide an acceptable fit is the probability that the diameter of a randomly selected cork is between 0.99 and 1.03 inches, which is:
P(0.99 < D < 1.03)
= P(0.99−1.00/0.05 < Z < 1.03−1.00/0.05)
= P(−0.2 < Z < 0.6)
≈ 0.305

= 0.91

Atticus assessed the accuracy of each throw by measuring the distance, in feet, from where the disc landed to the target. The results and summary statistics are shown in the following table.

Solution

Section 1:

Let μS represent the mean distance that standard discs weighing 175 grams land from the target for all possible throws, and let μL represent the mean distance that lightweight discs weighing 150 grams land from the target for all possible

throws.

The null hypothesis is H₀: μS = μL and the alternative hypothesis is Ha: μS ≠ μL.

An appropriate test is a two-sample t-test for a difference in population means.

Scoring

Section 1 is scored as follows:

Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following three components:

States a correct null hypothesis of equal means AND a correct alternative hypothesis of unequal means

Provides sufficient context by including reference to:

· the two groups (types of discs),

AND

· populations (all possible throws for each type of disc),

AND

· the response variable (distance from the target, or accuracy)

Identifies a two-sample t-test for a difference in population means by name (e.g., “two-sample t-test”) or by formula

Partially correct (P) if the response does not meet the criteria for E but satisfies either component 1 and/or component 3.

Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Additional Notes:

· The elements of component 2 do not have to be satisfied with the statement of the hypotheses. They may be satisfied by work presented anywhere in the response, most likely by the statement of the conclusion.

· If the statement of the hypotheses refers to population means and the conclusion refers to sample means (or vice versa), then the population aspect of component 2 is not satisfied.

· A response that states the hypotheses in words (e.g., “the null hypothesis is that the means are equal, and the alternative hypothesis is that the means are not equal”) may satisfy component 1. Neither context nor the concept of population is required to satisfy component 1.

· To satisfy component 1, the hypotheses must be stated in terms of two means. Use of standard symbols for means, with group labels (such as μStandard and μLightweight, or μ₁ and μ₂, or X̄S and X̄L) is sufficient to satisfy component 1. Any other symbols must be clearly defined as a mean and include labels to indicate two different populations (but the labels do not need to reflect the context of standard discs and lightweight discs) in order for the response to satisfy component 1.

· The notation μStandard and μLightweight satisfies the group and population aspects of component 2, because it is standard notation for population means and it reflects the context of standard discs and lightweight discs.

· Without additional description that provides links to standard discs and lightweight discs, notation such as μ₁ and μ₂ does not satisfy the group aspect of component 2.

Statistics 2021Intl MCQ

· A response that states the hypotheses in words may satisfy some or all aspects of component 2. For example, “the null hypothesis is that the mean distance from the target for all possible throws of a standard disc is equal to the mean distance from the target for all possible throws of a lightweight disc and the alternative hypothesis is that the means are not equal”) satisfies all aspects of component 2.

· If the response clearly refers to sample means instead of population means using words or symbols (e.g., X_S and X_L, or μ_Standard and μ_Lightweight), then the population aspect of component 2 is not satisfied unless the symbols used are defined as population means.

· If the response identifies the correct test by name, but also states an incorrect formula or uses incorrect notation in the formula, then component 3 is not satisfied.

· A pooled two-sample t-test may satisfy component 3 if the response comments on the plausibility of the equal population variances assumption.

· If the response identifies a two-sample z-test for a difference in population means, then component 3 is not satisfied.

Confidence Interval Approach:

o For a response using a confidence interval approach, component 1 is not considered in scoring. Such a response should be scored as follows:

· Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies components 2 and 3.

· Partially correct (P) if the response does not meet the criteria for E and satisfies either component 2 or 3.

o A response that uses a two-sample t-interval for a difference in population means must demonstrate all three aspects (groups, population, and response variable) to satisfy component 2. For example, component 2 is satisfied if the response indicates that it is a confidence interval for the difference between the mean distance from the target for all possible throws of a standard disc and the mean distance from the target for all possible throws of a lightweight disc, even if the hypotheses are not stated.

o If a two-sample t-interval for a difference in population means is identified correctly by name or by formula, then component 3 is satisfied.

o If the response uses individual one-sample t-intervals for the two means, which is not a correct approach, then component 3 is not satisfied.

Matched-Pairs Approach:

o Because a response presenting a matched-pairs t-test approach cannot satisfy component 3, such a response cannot meet the criteria for essentially correct, E. If the response satisfies component 1, it may be scored P. Otherwise, it is scored I.

o A response that attempts a matched-pairs approach using a confidence interval may be scored P if component 2 is satisfied and the response indicates that the confidence interval is for the mean difference. Otherwise, it is scored I.

Solution

Section 2:

The randomization condition for performing the two-sample t-test for a difference in population means is satisfied because the 30 discs (15 standard and 15 lightweight) are thrown in a random order. The sample sizes are both 15, which is less than 30, however, the approximately symmetric shape of the dotplots of the sample data from both discs support an

assumption that the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_S - \bar{x}_L$ is approximately normal.

STANDARD

3.2  3.4  3.6  3.8  4.0  4.2  4.4  4.6  4.8

Distance from Target (feet)

LIGHTWEIGHT

3.2  3.4  3.6  3.8  4.0  4.2  4.4  4.6  4.8

Distance from Target (feet)

$t = \frac{\bar{x}_S - \bar{x}_L}{\sqrt{\frac{s_S^2}{n_S} + \frac{s_L^2}{n_L}}} = \frac{4.173 - 4.080}{\sqrt{\frac{(0.356)^2}{15} + \frac{(0.416)^2}{15}}} \approx 0.658.$

The value of the $t$-statistic is

The corresponding $p$-value, using an approximate degrees of freedom of 27.3, is $P(|t| > 0.658) \approx 0.516.$

Scoring

Section 2 is scored as follows:

Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following four components:

Checks the randomization condition by referring to the throwing of the discs in random order t

Checks that the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_S - \bar{x}_L$ is approximately normal or doesn't violate the assumption that it could be normal (e.g., by sketching and commenting on the approximately symmetric shape of the graphs of the sample data from both discs) OR states that this condition must be assumed because both sample sizes are less than 30

Correctly reports the value of the $t$-statistic

Correctly reports the $p$-value, consistent with the reported test statistic and stated alternative hypothesis

Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies only two or three of the four components.

Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Additional Notes:

· In order to satisfy the reference to the randomization condition in component 1, it is minimally acceptable to state "randomization – check." However, component 1 is not satisfied if the response implies that randomization was only used for one of the two types of discs or if the response implies that the discs were randomly sampled from a population of discs (because it is the order in which the discs were thrown that was randomized).

· The 10% condition (all possible throws of each type of disc >150) does not need to be checked because the discs thrown were not randomly sampled from the population of all such discs. Because the inference conclusion is based on random ordering, it is a randomized experiment and not a random sample.

· Values for the test statistic and $p$-values depend on how the degrees of freedom were determined. The following combinations of $t$-values, $p$-values all satisfy components 3 and 4, respectively:

o The degrees of freedom for $t \approx 0.658$ from the model solution were computed with the Satterthwaite approximation used by many calculators:
$$
d f = \frac{\left(\frac{s_{S}^{2}}{n_{S}} + \frac{s_{L}^{2}}{n_{L}}\right)^{2}}{\frac{1}{n_{S}-1}\left(\frac{s_{S}^{2}}{n_{S}}\right)^{2} + \frac{1}{n_{L}-1}\left(\frac{s_{L}^{2}}{n_{L}}\right)^{2}} = \frac{\left(\frac{(0.356)^{2}}{15} + \frac{(0.416)^{2}}{15}\right)^{2}}{\frac{1}{15-1}\left(\frac{(0.356)^{2}}{15}\right)^{2} + \frac{1}{15-1}\left(\frac{(0.416)^{2}}{15}\right)^{2}} \approx 27.3
$$
o Using $t \approx 0.658$ and $(15 - 1) = 14$ degrees of freedom, the resulting $p$-value is 0.521.
o Using a pooled $t$-test with $t \approx 0.658$ and 28 degrees of freedom (which would be appropriate because the standard errors for the two samples are similar), the pooled estimate of the standard error is 0.387 and the resulting $p$-value is 0.516.

· Discussion of degrees of freedom will be treated as extraneous in scoring for Section 2.

· If the response compares the value of the test statistic to a critical value instead of computing a $p$-value, then the critical value (2.051 for 27.3 df, 2.145 for 14 df, or 2.048 for 28 df), or a critical value consistent with the stated alternative hypothesis, satisfies component 4.

· If a one-tailed alternative hypothesis is stated, then the $p$-value must be consistent with the stated alternative hypothesis to satisfy component 4.

· A response that reports the correct value for the $t$-statistic but contains errors in supporting work may still satisfy component 3.

· If the response satisfies component 4, any supporting work for the $p$-value may be treated as extraneous.

Confidence Interval Approach:

o If the response correctly calculates a 95% two-sample $t$-interval for the difference between two population means, then both component 3 and component 4 are satisfied.

o Based on 27.3 degrees of freedom, the 95% confidence $t$-interval for the difference between two population means is
$$
(\bar{x}_{S} - \bar{x}_{L}) \pm t_{(27.3), 0.975} \sqrt{\frac{s_{S}^{2}}{n_{S}} + \frac{s_{L}^{2}}{n_{L}}} = (4.173 - 4.080) \pm (2.051) \sqrt{\frac{(0.416)^{2}}{15} + \frac{(0.356)^{2}}{15}}
= (-0.196,\ 0.383)
$$

o Based on $(15 - 1) = 14$ degrees of freedom, the 95% confidence $t$-interval for the difference between two population means is
$$
(\bar{x}_{S} - \bar{x}_{L}) \pm t_{(14), 0.975} \sqrt{\frac{s_{S}^{2}}{n_{S}} + \frac{s_{L}^{2}}{n_{L}}} = (4.173 - 4.080) \pm (2.145) \sqrt{\frac{(0.416)^{2}}{15} + \frac{(0.356)^{2}}{15}}
= (-0.210,\ 0.396)
$$

Based on a pooled estimate of the common standard deviation and 28 degrees of freedom, the 95% confidence $t$-interval for the difference between two population means is
$$
(\bar{x}_{S} - \bar{x}_{L}) \pm t_{(28), 0.975} \sqrt{s_{p}^{2} \left(\frac{1}{n_{S}} + \frac{1}{n_{L}}\right)} = (4.173 - 4.080) \pm (2.048) \sqrt{(0.387)^{2} \left(\frac{1}{15} + \frac{1}{15}\right)}
= (-0.196,\ 0.383)
$$

o If the response uses separate one-sample $t$-intervals for the individual means, which is not a correct approach, then component 3 is not satisfied but component 4 may be satisfied if both intervals are computed correctly.

Matched-Pairs t-test Approach:

o Because it is unclear how to match, a response using a matched-pairs t-test or a matched-pairs confidence interval may be scored P, if it satisfies both of the following components:

· Component 1 is satisfied if the response checks the randomness condition by referring to the throwing of the discs in random order

· Component 2 is satisfied if the response includes a graph of DIFFERENCES in accuracy between the two types of discs that clearly labels the differences in accuracy or distance as the response variable AND provides a comment that does not rule out the application of an approximately normal model for the sampling distribution of DIFFERENCES.

o Otherwise, the response is scored I.

Solution

Section 3:

Statistics 2021Intl FRQ

Because the p-value is greater than any commonly used significance level, such as α = 0.05 or α = 0.10, the null hypothesis should not be rejected. The data do not provide convincing statistical evidence that mean distance from the target for all possible throws of a standard disc is not equal to the mean distance from the target for all possible throws of a lightweight disc, i.e., there is not convincing statistical evidence that the player tended to have more accuracy with one disc weight than with the other.

Scoring

Part (c) is scored as follows:

Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following two components:

Provides a correct justification of the conclusion based on the size of the reported p-value (or a comparison of the value of the test statistic to an appropriate critical value)

States a correct conclusion about the two means consistent with the stated alternative hypothesis OR states a correct conclusion about the difference in means that answers the inference question.

Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies only one of the two components.

Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Additional Notes:

· Including proper context (identifying groups, the concept of population means, and referencing the response variable) is important in stating the conclusion. Context displayed in stating the conclusion is considered in scoring component 2 of Section 1.

· If the conclusion and justification are consistent with an incorrect p-value (or an incorrect value of the test statistic, or an incorrect confidence interval, or an incorrect critical value), the response may satisfy component 1 and component 2.

· If the statement of the conclusion is incorrect AND the justification is inconsistent with the stated conclusion, then neither component 1 nor component 2 is satisfied. For example, the response correctly indicates that the p-value is larger than any reasonable significance level and makes an incorrect conclusion that the null hypothesis should be rejected and there is sufficient evidence to conclude that the mean accuracies are different for the two types of discs. The statement about the p-value is correct, but the conclusion is incorrect and the conclusion is not justified by the statement about the p-value.

· If the response omits hypotheses, assume the correct alternative hypothesis, Ha: μS ≠ μL, was provided when scoring component 2.

· If the response includes a statement that is equivalent to accepting the null hypothesis (e.g., “we conclude that the mean accuracies, or the mean distances from the target, are the same for the standard disc and the lightweight disc”), then component 2 is not satisfied.

· If the conclusion includes a definitive statement (such as “this proves that we do not have enough evidence to claim a difference” … or ”there is no evidence…”, then component 2 is not satisfied.

· If the response includes an incorrect interpretation of the p-value, then the score for Section 3 is lowered from E to P or from P to I.

· The conclusion may reference whether or not the types of discs tend to have different accuracy, or that “we cannot conclude that the two types of discs have different tendencies.” This can be read as “central tendency,” and therefore can be interpreted as the population mean.

· If the response incorrectly identified a two-sample z-test for a difference in population means, then section 3 should be scored using the reported p-value (or comparison of the z-value to a critical value obtained from the standard normal distribution).

· Confidence Interval Approach:

o If the alternative hypothesis is specified correctly as H_a: μ_S ≠ μ_L, then component 1 is satisfied if the justification is based on whether 0 is included in the reported confidence interval.

o If no alternative hypothesis is specified in the response, then assume the correct alternative hypothesis is provided when scoring component 1.

o If the response includes an incorrect interpretation of the confidence interval, then the score for Section 3 is lowered from E to P or from P to I.

o If the response uses separate one-sample t-intervals for the individual means, which is not a correct approach, then component 1 is satisfied if the justification is based on whether the intervals overlap.

The following tables show summary statistics and computer output for a regression analysis of the sample.

|       | Mean     | Standard Deviation |

| Predictor | Coef     | SE        | T     | P      |

(ii) Interpret S = 4.2682 in the context of the problem.

course is obtained, explain how this information could be used to investigate the sampling distribution of the estimated mean final scores for all students with a pretest score of 65.

Solution

Part (a):

The equation of the least-squares regression line for predicting final score from pretest score is

ŷ = 49.2804 + 0.5396x, where x is the pretest score and ŷ is the predicted final score for students with a pretest score of x.

Scoring

Part (a) is scored as follows:

Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following three components:

Identifies correct values for the slope and intercept in the equation of the least-squares regression line

Identifies the final score as the response variable AND pretest score as the explanatory variable

The concept of a “predicted value” or “estimated mean” is expressed either with words (e.g., “predicted final score”) OR using the ŷ symbol in the equation

Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies component 2 AND component 3 but interchanges the values of the slope and intercept in the equation

OR

if the response satisfies component 1 AND only one of the other two components.

Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Additional Notes:

· In identifying the equation of the least-squares regression line, any symbols may be used for the variables.

· A response that reports an equation of the form (final score) = 49.2804 + 0.5396(pretest score) satisfies component 1 and component 2.

· A response that reports an equation of the form (predicted final score) = 49.2804 + 0.5396(pretest score) or final score = 49.2804 + 0.5396(pretest score) satisfies all three components and is scored E.

A response that reports an equation of the form (predicted final score) = 0.5396 + 49.2804(pretest score) satisfies the first set of criteria for P, but a response that reports an equation of the form (final score) = 0.5396 + 49.2804(pretest score) does not satisfy components 1 or 3 and is scored I.

Solution

Part (b):

(i) The value of the predicted final score for students with a pretest score of 65 is 49.2804 + 0.5396(65) ≈ 84.354.

(ii) The value S = 4.2682 is the standard deviation of the residuals from the regression for predicting final score from pretest score. When the regression line is used to predict final score, a typical prediction error would be about 4.2682.

Scoring

Part (b) is scored as follows:

Statistics 2021Intl FRQ

Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following two components:

Correctly calculates the value of the predicted final score for students with a pretest score of 65 in part (b-i) AND provides supporting work for the calculation of the value in part (b-i) using the equation identified in part (a)

Provides a correct interpretation in context of S = 4.2682 as a typical prediction error (difference between an observed and predicted final score) OR provides a correct definition in context of S = 4.2682 as the standard deviation (or estimate of the standard deviation) of the residuals.

Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies only one of the two components OR if the response to part (b-i) substitutes the value 65 into the correct equation, or an incorrect equation identified in part (a), but does not correctly calculate the value of the prediction AND provides a weak interpretation of S = 4.2682 in part (b-ii) OR if the response to part (b-i) correctly calculates the value of the prediction without supporting work AND provides a weak interpretation of S = 4.2682 in part (b-ii).

Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Additional Notes:

· Acceptable alternate wordings for interpretation of S = 4.2682 in part (b-ii):

· S = 4.2682 is the standard deviation (or the estimate of the standard deviation) of all final scores for students with a particular pretest score x

· A typical difference between an observed final score and the predicted final score is about S = 4.2682

· On average, we expect the difference between a predicted final score and the actual final score to be about S = 4.2682

· A difference between an observed final score and the predicted final score is about S = 4.2682

· A response that includes a correct definition of S in context and includes an interpretation of S that is considered weak still satisfies component 2

· An interpretation of S = 4.2682 is considered weak, if it exhibits at least one of the following characteristics:

· It does not reference final exam scores (no context)

· The interpretation refers to “prediction error” or “residual” without a qualifier such as “typical” or “on average”

· A response that includes a correct definition of S in context but includes an incorrect interpretation of S does not satisfy component 2.

Solution

Part (c):

Point Estimate II has the larger sampling variability. The sampling variability for Point Estimate I reflects how much the value on the least-squares regression line for a particular pretest score varies across all possible random samples of 12 students. The sampling variability for Point Estimate II is larger because it reflects student-to-student variability in final scores among all students with the same particular pretest score in addition to the sampling variability of Point Estimate I.

Scoring

Part (c) is scored as follows:

Essentially correct

options.E: if the response satisfies the following two components:
options.A: OR
options.B: are:

text: Select a random sample of 12 students from the list of students who completed the course.
Compute a least-squares regression line using the information for the 12 students in the sample and use it to predict the final score for a student with a pretest score of 65.

Repeat the first two steps a large number of times, say M times.

Solution 2
From the list of all students who complete the course, create a second list of all students with a pretest score of 65. Select a random sample of 12 students from the second list.
Record the mean final score for the 12 students in the sample.

Repeat the first two steps a large number of times, say M times.

The resulting list of M values constitutes the simulated sampling distribution of estimated mean final score for all students with a pretest score of 65.

Scoring

Part (d) is scored as follows:

Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following three components:

Describes selecting a random sample of 12 students from: the list of students who completed the course (Solution 1); OR from the list of all students with a pretest score of 65 (Solution 2).

Computes a least-squares regression line for the generated sample AND uses it to predict the final score for a student with a pretest score of 65 (Solution 1)

Calculates the mean final score of the 12 students in the generated sample (Solution 2)

Indicates that the steps described in components 1 and 2 should be performed a large number of times

Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies only two of the three components.

Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Additional Notes:

· In order to satisfy component 1, the response must include the following:

o Reference taking random samples of 12 students

AND

o Identify from what list the samples are being drawn.

· A response that describes taking all samples of size 12 (from either list) to construct the sampling distribution rather than random samples of size 12 has not addressed simulating the sampling distribution and therefore does not satisfy component 1, but does satisfy component 3.

· In order to satisfy component 2 using Solution 1, the response must indicate that a regression analysis is performed for the generated sample and used to compute the predicted final score for a student with a pretest score of 65. Note that the pretest score of 65 must be referenced.

· In order to satisfy component 2 using Solution 2, the response must indicate that the mean final score is calculated for the generated sample of 12 students.

Parts (a), (b), (c), and (d) are each scored as essentially correct (E), partially correct (P), or incorrect (I).

AP
CollegeBoard
Scoring Guide
Statistics 2021Intl MCQ

0     1     2     3     4

Parts (a) through (d) sum to 4 points

OR

Parts (a) through (d) sum to 3½ points AND a holistic approach is used to decide to score up

Part (a) essentially correct
Part (a) partially correct
Part (a) incorrect
Part (b) essentially correct
Part (b) partially correct
Part (b) incorrect
Part (c) essentially correct
Part (c) partially correct
Part (c) incorrect
Part (d) essentially correct
Part (d) partially correct
Part (d) incorrect

Solution

Part (a):

This study is an experiment. The researchers imposed a treatment (being shown a video in which someone yawned, or being shown a video in which no one yawned) on each volunteer in the study. Moreover, since the volunteers were randomly assigned to the treatments, this is a completely randomized experiment.

Scoring

Part (a) is scored as follows:

Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following three components:

Identifies the study as an experiment (or completely randomized experiment)

Provides a valid explanation that indicates that the researchers imposed the treatments

Provides sufficient context, by including reference to the subjects (volunteers) AND the two treatments (being shown a video in which someone yawned and being shown a video in which no one yawned; or Group A and Group B)

Statistics 2021Intl FRQ

Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies only two of the three components.
Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Additional Notes:
· A response may satisfy component 2 by indicating that the volunteers were assigned (or randomly assigned), or not allowed to select their treatment to the two treatment groups.

A response that provides an explanation that only refers to the use of randomization but does not also refer to the imposition of treatments on the volunteers (e.g., “this is an experiment because the researchers used randomization”) does not satisfy component 2. However, a response that specifically refers to the researchers’ use of “random assignment” (e.g., “this is an experiment because the researchers used random assignment”) may satisfy component 2 but may not satisfy component 3 without sufficient context.

Solution

Part (b):

In order to use the two-sample z-test for a difference in population proportions to test the given hypotheses, the sample sizes must be sufficiently large enough to ensure that the sampling distribution of  \hat{p_A} - \hat{p_B}  is approximately normal. In other words, for the combined sample, the combined (or pooled) proportion  \hat{p_c} = \frac{n_A \hat{p_A} + n_B \hat{p_B}}{n_A + n_B}  is used to verify that each of  n_A \hat{p_c}, n_A (1 - \hat{p_c}), n_B \hat{p_c},  and  n_B (1 - \hat{p_c})  are all greater than or equal to 10 (or 5). Since there were only 16 volunteers in Group B and the combined (or pooled) proportion of volunteers who yawned was  \hat{p_c} = \frac{11+3}{34+16} = \frac{14}{50} = 0.28,  the expected number of volunteers who would yawn in Group B is  n_B \hat{p_c} = (16)(0.28) = 4.48.  Because this expected count is less than 10 (or 5), the sample sizes are not sufficiently large enough to ensure that the sampling distribution of  \hat{p_A} - \hat{p_B}  is approximately normal.

Scoring

Part (b) is scored as follows:

Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following two components:

Explains that a condition for using the two-sample z-test for a difference in population proportions is that:

· The sampling distribution of  \hat{p_A} - \hat{p_B}  is approximately normal

OR

·  n(\hat{p_c})  and  n(1 - \hat{p_c})  should be greater than 10 (or 5) for BOTH groups

Correctly calculates the expected number of volunteers who would yawn in Group B as  (16)(0.28) = 4.48

Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies only one of the two components.

Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Additional Notes:

The second bullet of component 1 may be satisfied by indicating that the observed counts for BOTH groups,  n_A \hat{p_A},  n_A (1 - \hat{p_A}),  n_B \hat{p_B},  and  n_B (1 - \hat{p_B}),  must all be greater than 10 (or 5).

· Component 1 is not satisfied if the response indicates that it is checking for some condition other than the normality condition for the sampling distribution of  \hat{p_A} - \hat{p_B}  (e.g. checking for independence). Other references to independence are considered extraneous.

Statistics 2021 Intl MCQ

· Component 2 may be satisfied by identifying Group B as the group with an observed count of 3.

Solution

Part (c):

The point estimate for $p_A - p_B$ is the estimated difference in proportions, or the observed difference in the sample proportions, $\hat{p}_A - \hat{p}_B = \frac{11}{34} - \frac{3}{16} \approx 0.136$.

Scoring

Part (c) is scored as follows:

Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following two components:

Correctly calculates the point estimate for $p_A - p_B$ as 0.136

Provides supporting work for the calculation of the point estimate for $p_A - p_B$

Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies only one of the two components

OR

if the response shows the correct proportions but reverses the order of the subtraction resulting in an incorrect value of the point estimate of $-0.136$.

Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Additional Notes:

· $\frac{11}{34} - \frac{3}{16} = \frac{74}{544}$ or $\frac{11}{34} - \frac{3}{16}$ or $0.3235 - 0.1875$ satisfies both component 1 and component 2.

· When calculating the value 0.136, ignore minor arithmetic errors or transcription errors if they can be identified by the work shown

Solution

Part (d):

(i) Using the point estimate for $p_A - p_B$ from part (c) of 0.136 and the simulated results, the approximate $p$-value for the test stated in part (b) is $0.171 + 0.081 + 0.016 = 0.268$.

(ii) Because the $p$-value from part (d-i) is greater than the significance level of 0.05, the null hypothesis from part (b) $H_0: p_A = p_B$ should not be rejected. There is not convincing statistical evidence that the proportion of all people who would yawn when shown the video in which someone yawned is greater than the proportion of all people who would yawn when shown the video in which no one yawned. Therefore, the study does not provide convincing evidence that yawning is contagious.

Scoring

Part (d) is scored as follows:

Essentially correct (E) if the response satisfies the following four components:

Correctly calculates the $p$-value in part (d-i)

Provides appropriate supporting work for the calculation of the $p$-value in part (d-i)

Statistics 2021Intl FRQ

Indicates that the data do not provide sufficient evidence to conclude that yawning is contagious.

Provides a correct justification for the conclusion based on the size of the p-value calculated in part (d-i).

Partially correct (P) if the response satisfies only two or three of the four components.

Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P.

Additional Notes:

· A response that excludes the bar from the graph at 0.136 in the calculation of the p-value in part (d-i) does not satisfy component 1 since the p-value is the probability of obtaining a test statistic as extreme or more extreme than the observed test statistic, when the null hypothesis and probability model are assumed to be true. However, such a response may satisfy component 2 if the response provides the supporting work 0.081 + 0.016 = 0.097.

· A response that rounds the decimal approximations of the proportions from the graph to two places (e.g., 0.17 + 0.08 + 0.02 = 0.27) in the calculation of the p-value in part (d-i) may still satisfy component 1 and component 2.

· A response that incorrectly calculates the p-value in part (d-i) may still satisfy components 2 through 4 if the response is consistent with the use of the incorrect p-value.

A popular computer card game keeps track of the number of games played and the number of games won on that 
computer. The cards are shuffled before each game, so the outcome of the game is independent from one game 
to the next and is based on the skill of the player. Let X represent the number of games that have been won out 
of 100 games. Under which of the following situations would X be a binomial random variable?

options.A: All games were played by the same player, whose skill improved over the course of the 100 games.
options.B: A group of 5 players of different skill levels were each allowed to play 20 games in a row.
options.C:
A group of players of different skill levels were each allowed to play until they had lost 3 games and this 
resulted in 100 games played.
options.D:
Two players of equal skill level each played one game a day for 50 days and their skill level did not change 
from day to day.
options.E:
Two players of different skill levels competed by allowing one player to continue until a game was lost, 
then the other player to continue until a game was lost, and so on, until 100 games were played.

A pollster is interested in comparing the proportions of women and men in a particular town who are in favor  
of a ban on fireworks within town borders. The pollster plans to test the hypothesis that the proportion of women 
in favor of the ban is different from the proportion of men in favor of the ban. There are 4,673 women and  
4,502 men who live in the town. From a simple random sample of 40 women in the town, the pollster finds that 
38 favor the ban. From an independent simple random sample of 50 men in the town, the pollster finds that  
27 favor the ban. Which of the following statements is true about this situation?

options.A: Because the samples are from normal populations, a two-proportion z-test would be valid.
options.B: Because the size of each sample is greater than 30, a two-proportion z-test would be valid.
options.C:
Because the number who favored the ban is greater than 10 in both groups, a two-proportion z-test would be 
valid.
options.D: Because of the relative sizes of the populations and samples, a two-proportion z-test would be valid.
options.E: A two-proportion z-test would not be valid for these data.

text:
A researcher constructed a 95 percent confidence interval for the mean number of alfalfa weevils on an alfalfa 
plant within a field. Based on 80 randomly selected alfalfa plants, the researcher found an average of 2.5 alfalfa 
weevils per plant and computed the 95 percent confidence interval to be 1.50 to 3.50. Which of the following 
statements is a correct interpretation of the 95 percent confidence level?

options.A:
Approximately 95 percent of alfalfa fields sampled will have an average of 1.50 to 3.50 alfalfa weevils per 
plant, but nothing can be said about the sample mean number of alfalfa weevils for this or any other field.
options.B:
Approximately 95 percent of alfalfa fields sampled will have an average of 1.50 to 3.50 alfalfa weevils per 
plant. The sample mean for this field was 2.50 alfalfa weevils per plant, but the sample means for other 
fields may be different.
options.C:
If we repeatedly sampled this field, taking samples of 80 plants and constructing 95% confidence intervals, 
then, approximately 95 percent of these intervals would include 2.5, the mean for the sample described 
above.
options.D:
If we repeatedly sampled this field, taking samples of 80 plants and constructing 95% confidence intervals, 
then, approximately 95 percent of these intervals would include the population mean number of alfalfa 
weevils on an alfalfa plant in this field.
options.E:
If we repeatedly sampled this field, taking samples of 80 plants and constructing 95% confidence intervals, 
then, approximately 95 percent of these intervals would include the sample mean for that sample.

text:
A large clinical trial was designed to determine whether a certain vitamin improves the general health of adults. 
The investigators first identified 85 variables that measure various aspects of the general health of adults. 
Because each adult in the clinical trial was to serve as his or her own control, the 85 variables were measured for 
each adult, both before taking the vitamin and after taking the vitamin for three months. The investigators then 
performed 85 matched-pair t-tests, one for each variable. They found statistically significant results at the 0.05 
level in 2 of the variables, both in the direction of improved general health. Which of the following should the 
investigators conclude?

options.A:
There is evidence that the vitamin improves the health of adults because 2 of the 85 tests were statistically 
significant.
options.B:
There is evidence that the vitamin improves the health of adults because in clinical trials investigators 
typically underestimate the proportion of the population that is helped by a vitamin.
options.C:
There is insufficient evidence that the vitamin improves the health of adults because at the 0.05 significance 
level, one could easily get statistically significant results in 2 out of 85 tests just due to chance variability.
options.D:
There is insufficient evidence that the vitamin improves the health of adults because the sample size of 85 is 
not large enough to draw a conclusion.
options.E: No conclusion can be drawn because an even number of variables is needed for a matched-pairs design.

is false. If a significance level of 0.01 were to be used instead of a significance level of 0.05, which of the
following would be true?

options.A: Neither the probability of a Type II error nor the power of the test would change.
options.B: Both the probability of a Type II error and the power of the test would decrease.
options.C: Both the probability of a Type II error and the power of the test would increase.
options.D: The probability of a Type II error would decrease and the power of the test would increase.
options.E: The probability of a Type II error would increase and the power of the test would decrease.

text: Which of the following distinguishes an observational study from a randomized experiment?

options.A:
In an observational study volunteers are always used, whereas in a randomized experiment a random sample 
is always taken from the population.
options.B:
In an observational study a random sample is always taken from the population, whereas in a randomized 
experiment volunteers are always used.
options.C:
In an observational study treatments are not randomly assigned, whereas in a randomized experiment 
treatments are randomly assigned.
options.D:
In an observational study a control group is never used, whereas in a randomized experiment a control group 
is always used.
options.E:
An observational study can be double-blind, whereas a randomized experiment can only be single-blind 
because the experimenter determines who is randomly assigned to each treatment.

text:
Frank, who lives in Texas, and his sister Lilly, who lives in Japan, correspond regularly. From what he can tell 
from the postmarks on both his and his sister’s letters, it appears that it takes longer for Lilly’s mail from Japan 
to reach him in Texas than it does for his letters from Texas to reach her in Japan. When Frank called his post 
office to ask if there was a reason for this, the postmaster told him that the delivery time of letters in both 
directions should be the same. Frank and his sister decided to collect data to see if letters from Japan to Texas 
take longer to be delivered than letters from Texas to Japan. They recorded the delivery time in days. After 
convincing themselves that the assumptions were reasonable, they performed a two-sample t-test and obtained 
the following computer output.  
Two sample T for To Texas vs To Japan
 
N 
Mean 
StDev 
SE Mean
To Texas 
12 
8.74 
2.92 
0.84 
To Japan 
9 
6.75 
2.56 
0.85 
95% CI for mu To Texas - mu To Japan: (-0.53, 4.51) 
T-Test mu To Texas = mu To Japan (vs >): T = 1.66   P = 0.058   DF = 18
 Using a significance level of 0.05, which of the following statements best describes the conclusion that can be 
drawn from these data?

options.A: There is convincing evidence that there is no difference in the mean delivery times.
options.B: There is convincing evidence that there is a difference in the mean delivery times.
options.C:
There is convincing evidence that the mean delivery time from Japan to Texas is greater than the mean 
delivery time from Texas to Japan.
options.D:
There is not convincing evidence that the mean delivery time from Japan to Texas is greater than the mean 
delivery time from Texas to Japan.
options.E: The t-test cannot be used for sample sizes that are this small.

Researchers investigated the possible beneficial effect on heart health of drinking black tea and whether adding 
milk to the tea reduces any possible benefit. Twenty-four volunteers were randomly assigned to one of three 
groups. Every day for a month, participants in group 1 drank two cups of hot black tea without milk, participants 
in group 2 drank two cups of hot black tea with milk, and participants in group 3 drank two cups of hot water but 
no tea. At the end of the month, the researchers measured the change in each of the participants’ heart health. 
(a) Did the researchers conduct an experiment or an observational study? Explain. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
(b) Why did the researchers include a group who drank hot water but no tea? 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
(c) Is it reasonable to generalize the results of the study beyond the 24 participants? Explain why or why not.

 6. The United States one-cent coin is called a penny. Banks often use a machine to count and package pennies in 
rolls of 50 pennies. Occasionally a machine malfunctions and begins putting fewer than 50 pennies in each roll. 
The quality control inspector at a certain bank wants to investigate whether its machine is functioning properly 
but does not want to break open the rolls to count pennies. The inspector will measure the weight of a roll of 
pennies to test the following hypotheses. 
0
H
: The machine is putting 50 pennies in each roll.
Ha : The machine is putting fewer than 50 pennies in each roll. 
(a) For testing the hypotheses above, describe what a Type II error would be and describe a consequence of 
making a Type II error. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Description of error: 
Consequence:
For pennies produced since 1983, the distribution of weight is approximately normal with a mean of 2.5 grams 
and a standard deviation of 0.04 gram. Assume that the pennies in a roll can be considered a random sample of 
pennies produced since 1983. 
  Let the random variable W represent the total weight of a roll of pennies. Ignore the weight of the packaging of 
the roll. 
(b) Describe the distribution of W if the selected roll contains 50 pennies.

The inspector will use the following rule for each selected roll. 
 
If the total weight of the roll of pennies is 124 grams or less, I will
conclude that the roll contains fewer than 50 pennies. Otherwise,  
I will conclude that the roll contains 50 pennies. 
 
(c) Suppose the selected roll contains 50 pennies. Using the inspector’s rule, what is the probability that the 
inspector will conclude that the selected roll contains fewer than 50 pennies? 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
(d) Suppose the selected roll actually contains 49 pennies. Using the inspector’s rule, is the inspector likely to 
conclude that the selected roll contains fewer than 50 pennies? Justify your answer. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
(e) Based on your answers to (c) and (d), comment on whether the inspector’s rule will be effective at 
distinguishing a roll that contains 50 pennies from a roll that contains fewer than 50 pennies.

Free-Response Scoring Guidelines 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The following contains the scoring guidelines 
for the free-response questions in this exam.

AP® STATISTICS 
2014 SCORING GUIDELINES 
 
Question 1 
 
Intent of Question  
 
The primary goals of this question were to assess a student’s ability to (1) interpret and summarize 
information from a graph; and (2) assess differences in variability between two groups from a graph. 
 
Solution 
 
Part (a): 
 
Similarities: Both cities have the same pattern of average monthly temperatures throughout the year.  
More specifically, both cities are colder in the winter months at the beginning and end of the year and 
warmer in the summer months during the middle part of the year.  
 
Differences: Madison is warmer during more months of the year than Juneau. Madison’s average 
monthly temperatures vary more than Juneau’s. For example, at the beginning and end of the year 
Madison’s temperatures are lower than Juneau’s but from April to November Madison temperatures 
are higher than Juneau’s. 
 
Part (b): 
 
Madison has the greater standard deviation of average monthly temperatures because Madison’s 
temperatures deviate more from its mean than Juneau’s temperatures deviate from its corresponding 
mean. Standard deviation is (roughly) the average deviation from the mean, and Madison’s average 
monthly temperatures tend to vary from the mean much more than Juneau’s do. 
 
Scoring 
 
This question was scored in three sections. Section 1 consists of the description of the similarities in part 
(a); section 2 consists of the description of the differences in part (a); and section 3 consists of part (b). 
Each section was scored as essentially correct (E), partially correct (P), or incorrect (I). 
 
Section 1 is scored as follows: 
 
Essentially correct (E) if the response states that both cities are cold in both the beginning and end of 
the year AND both cities are warm in months in the middle part of the year; 
OR 
if the response states that the temperatures for both cities increase from January to July and decrease 
from July to December. 
 
Partially correct (P) if the response describes similarities between the two cities in at least two months 
(for example, both cities are hottest in July, both cities are coldest in January, the cities have similar 
temperatures in March); 
OR 
if the response states that both cities are cold in both the beginning and end of the year OR that both 
cities are warm in months in the middle part of the year, but not both; 
OR 
if the response states that temperatures in both cities increase and then decrease without identifying 
July as the peak.

Question 1 (continued) 
 
Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for an E or a P. 
 
Note: If the response mentions normality then the score is reduced one level (that is, from E to P or  
from P to I).  
 
Section 2 is scored as follows: 
 
Essentially correct (E) if the response includes the following two components:

A correct comparison of the centers; for example, Madison has a tendency for higher  
average monthly temperatures overall, or Madison is warmer than Juneau during more  
months of the year (April to November).

A correct comparison of the variabilities; for example, Madison has greater variability  
than Juneau. 
 
Partially correct (P) if the response includes only one of the two components listed above. 
Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for an E or P; 
OR 
only discusses differences month-by-month without providing a larger summary 
 
 
Section 3 is scored as follows:

Question 4 (continued) 
 
Scoring 
 
Part (a), (b) and (c) were scored as essentially correct (E), partially correct (P), or incorrect (I). 
 
Part (a) is scored as follows: 
 
Essentially correct (E) if the response correctly performs the calculation AND shows a correct method 
of solution. 
 
Partially correct (P) if the response indicates knowledge to use the multiplication rule but multiplies 
two wrong, but reasonable, values based on information from the stem of the problem (for example,

0.7 ). 
 
Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for an E or a P, including if the response gives 
the correct answer with no work shown. 
 
Part (b) is scored as follows: 
 
Essentially correct (E) if the response correctly calculates all three probabilities AND shows sufficient 
work for at least two of the probabilities, which can include work coming from part (a) or part (c). 
 
Note: If the answer to part (a) is not correct and is carried forward to part b), then the response to part 
(b) can earn an E if one additional probability is calculated correctly with supporting work and the four 
probabilities sum to 1.  
 
Partially correct (P) if the response correctly calculates at least two of the three probabilities but shows 
sufficient work for only one of the probabilities, which can include work coming from part (a) or part (c); 
OR 
if the response correctly calculates only one of the three probabilities AND shows sufficient work, 
which can include work coming from part (a) or part (c) AND reports probabilities that sum to 1;  
OR 
if the responses calculates geometric probabilities AND shows work AND makes the probabilities sum 
to 1. 
 
Note: The response in part b) can receive a score of P if the response correctly calculates all three 
probabilities with no supporting work shown.  
 
Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for an E or a P.

Question 5 
 
Intent of Question  
 
The primary goals of this question were to assess a student’s ability to: (1) interpret information displayed 
in comparative bar graphs; (2) identify an appropriate statistical test to address a research question; and 
(3) draw an appropriate conclusion from a test statistic. 
 
Solution 
 
Part (a): 
 
The graph reveals that males and females tend to differ with regard to which is their favorite sport to 
watch. Males tend to select football and basketball more often than females, whereas females tend  
to select gymnastics and swimming more often than males. The relative frequencies are similar for 
baseball between males and females.  
 
Part (b): 
 
The appropriate statistical test is a chi-squared test for independence. The number of degrees of 
freedom is (

Part (c):  
 
The p-value, based on a chi-squared test statistic of 10.77 and determined using technology, is 
approximately 0.029. This p-value is smaller than the significance level of 0.05. Therefore, the sample 
data provides convincing statistical evidence that there is an association between gender and sport 
preference in the population of all students at this school.

Scoring 
 
Parts (a), (b), and (c) were scored as essentially correct (E), partially correct (P), or incorrect (I): 
 
Part (a) is scored as follows: 
 
Essentially correct (E) if the response correctly identifies at least one sport for which males exhibit  
a higher relative frequency than females, and at least one sport for which females exhibit a higher 
relative frequency than males;  
OR  
correctly identifies at least one sport for which the relative frequencies for males and females are 
different, and at least one sport for which the relative frequencies for males and females are similar;  
OR  
correctly identifies at least two differences between the distributions of sport preferences for males  
and females. 
 
Partially correct (P) if the response correctly identifies at least one sport for which males have a  
higher relative frequency or at least one sport for which females have a higher relative frequency, but 
not both; 
OR  
correctly identifies one difference between the distributions of sport preferences for males and females. 
 
Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for an E or a P 
 
Notes:

An ambiguous statements such as “Males like football more while females like gymnastics 
more” is interpreted as contrasting gender differences across different sports (that is, males 
like football more [than females do] while females like gymnastics more [than males do]).

A response containing incorrect statistical statements can be scored no higher than P. 
 
Part (b) is scored as follows: 
 
Essentially correct (E) if the response correctly provides the following three components:
Name of test
Identification of the two-variable nature of the test

Degrees of freedom 
 
Partially correct (P) if the response correctly provides two of the three components listed above.  
 
Incorrect (I) if the response correctly provides at most one of the three components listed above.

It is not necessary for the response to include supporting work for the calculation of the 
degrees of freedom. However if supporting work is shown, it must be correct.

Question 6 
 
Intent of Question 
 
The primary goals of this question were to assess a student’s ability to: (1) describe Type II error and a 
consequence in context; (2) determine the sampling distribution for a total and use it to determine a test 
procedure; and (3) perform and interpret a normal probability calculation related to a hypothesis test. 
 
Solution 
 
Part (a): 
 
Description: Type II error would be failing to reject the null hypothesis that the machine is putting  
50 pennies in each roll when in reality the machine is putting fewer than 50 pennies in each roll.   
 
Consequence: A consequence would be that the machine would not be repaired even though it is 
malfunctioning, so the machine would continue to put fewer than 50 pennies in each roll. 
 
Part (b):

correctly conclude that the roll contains fewer than 50 pennies.

AP® STATISTICS 
2014 SCORING GUIDELINES 
 
Question 6 (continued) 
 
Part (e): 
 
The inspector’s rule is very effective for distinguishing a roll that contains 50 pennies from one  
that contains fewer than 50 pennies. Part (c) reveals that if the roll actually contains 50 pennies,  
the probability is only 0.0002 that the inspector will mistakenly conclude that the roll contains fewer 
than 50 pennies. Part (d) reveals that if the roll actually contains fewer than 50 pennies, then it is 
extremely likely that the inspector will correctly conclude that the roll contains fewer than 50 pennies. 
Therefore, whether the roll contains 50 pennies or fewer, the inspector’s rule is extremely likely to  
lead to the correct decision. 
 
Scoring 
 
This question was scored in four sections. Section 1 consists of part (a); section 2 consists of parts (b)  
and (c) combined; section 3 consists of part (d); and section 4 consists of part (e). Each section was  
scored as essentially correct (E), partially correct (P), or incorrect (I). 
 
Section 1 is scored as follows: 
 
Essentially correct (E) if the response includes the following two components:
Correct description of Type II error

Correct consequence of the error in context 
 
Partially correct (P) if the response includes only one of the two components listed above; 
OR 
if the response includes both components, but for a Type I error. 
 
Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for an E or a P. 
 
Section 2 is scored as follows: 
 
Essentially correct (E) if the response includes the following three components:
Correct description of the shape of the sampling distribution as approximately normal

Correct calculations, with work shown, of the mean and standard deviation of the  
sampling distribution

Correct calculation of the relevant probability 
 
Partially correct (P) if the response includes two of the three components listed above. 
 
Incorrect (I) if the response includes at most one of the three components listed above. 
 
Section 3 is scored as follows: 
 
Essentially correct (E) if the response includes the following two components:
Correct calculations of the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution

Correct conclusion that answers the question, with linkage to a z-score calculation,  
or a probability, or similar reasonable justification

The College Board 
The College Board is a mission-driven not-for-profit organization that connects students to college 
success and opportunity. Founded in 1900, the College Board was created to expand access to 
higher education. Today, the membership association is made up of over 6,000 of the world’s leading 
educational institutions and is dedicated to promoting excellence and equity in education. Each year, 
the College Board helps more than seven million students prepare for a successful transition to college 
through programs and services in college readiness and college success ----- including the SAT® and  
the Advanced Placement Program®. The organization also serves the education community through 
research and advocacy on behalf of students, educators, and schools. The College Board is committed  
to the principles of excellence and equity, and that commitment is embodied in all of its programs, 
services, activities, and concerns.

A study of the effect of note-taking on student learning used two groups, each
consisting of 100 biology students.
· Group 1: On day one of the study, these 100 students were given an hour-long
class on how to take notes in outline form.
· Group 2: On day one of the study, these 100 students were not given any
training on note-taking.
On day two of the study, both groups were placed in a classroom and were taught a
lesson on biology. After the lesson, the students were given an hour to review their
notes and then took an assessment on the content of the lesson. The students in
Group 1 performed significantly better on the assessment than the students in Group
2 did.

Which of the following statements about the study is true？
A.
The explanatory variable is the performance on the assessment.
B.
The response variable is whether training was received on taking outline
notes.
C.
The experimental unit is the assessment.
D.
The treatment is the lesson on biology.
E.
A confounding variable could be whether students are good at taking
assessments.

According to a report the mean number of rolls of toilet paper used each year by
residents of a certain city is 100. To investigate this claim, researchers surveyed a
random sample of residents from the city and recorded the number of rolls of toilet
paper they used each year. They used the data to test the hypotheses H0 : µ ＝100
versus Ha : µ ≠100，where µ is the mean number of rolls of toilet paper used each
year by all residents of the city.

The conditions for inference were met and the p-value of the test is 0.058. At
the significance level of α ＝0.05, which of the following statements is true?
A.
There is convincing statistical evidence that the mean number of rolls of
toilet paper used each year by all residents of the city is 100.
B.
There is convincing statistical evidence that the mean number of rolls of
toilet paper used each year by all residents of the city is different than
100.
C.
The researchers should reject that the mean number of rolls of toilet
paper used each year by all residents of the city is 100.
D.
There is not convincing statistical evidence that the mean number of rolls
of toilet paper used each year by all residents of the city is different than
100.
E.
The researchers failed to obtain any evidence that the mean number of
rolls of toilet paper used each year by all residents of the city is different
than 100.

Which of the following confidence intervals should the researcher use for the

A one-sample t-interval for estimating a population mean

A matched-pairs t-interval for estimating a mean difference
D.
A two-sample t-interval for estimating a difference between sample
means

A two-sample t-interval for estimating a difference between population
